author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/test/0/test_90
body:
Why do so many clever women, some of them with degrees, gifts, families and reputations to protect, freely choose to spend their working lives in the City of London? Perhaps it is the money. Perhaps it is the challenge. Perhaps, like the mountaineer, they simply want to prove it can be done. Whatever the motivation, one thing has become abundantly clear from the parade of discrimination and unfair-dismissal claims which now constitute our principal source of knowledge about the place: the City is a brutish, sexist environment in which no sensible woman would wish to linger.

Not, of course, as brutish as some police forces, or fast-food chains, or the sort of men's magazine that finds a woman's cleavage funnier than her opinions, or the average mini-brothel, or, when it comes down to it, the General Synod of the Church of England. But brutish enough. And were it not for the aforementioned claims, with their invaluable disclosures, the City might still be passing itself off, in its brochures, as a model equal-opportunities employer, no more hostile to the ambitious woman than a branch of Boots.

The tradition of the City complainant is a noble one, beginning, in its modern form, with Nicola Horlick in 1997. Mrs Horlick, it will be remembered, contrived to transform what was, at bottom, a grubby dispute about the poaching of staff into a full-blown moral crusade, dragging a party of entranced reporters all the way to Frankfurt and back in pursuit of her wronged honour. That she recovered from this ordeal with quite remarkable speed did nothing to diminish the example she set. Her successors have merely demanded ever-larger sums, to compensate them for the bullying, the spitting, the name-calling ("hot totty" being a favourite), and the various lapdancing incidents which appear to be a feature of City life.

Consider Claire Bright, who seeks £11m from HBOS, where she was making do on a mere £600,000, having allegedly suffered under a man she memorably christened "a mini Hitler". One admires the restraint of the phrase. Given the modest dimensions of a City office, as against those of Nazi Germany, only a mini-Holocaust was ever realistically achievable, and it seems churlish to hold this against her. Or consider the six Dresdner Kleinwort Wasserstein bankers who between them demanded £800m, in recompense for offences which included one of their number being called "the Pamela Anderson of trading", and the collective trauma of watching a chief executive date his assistant.

It is at this point that a certain vulgar arithmetician might be tempted to reach for the figures paid out by the Criminal Injuries Compensation Board to the victims of the July 7 bombings — roughly £50,000, one gathers, per lost leg. But such comparisons are surely beneath us. Indeed, one begins to wonder whether newspapers ought to advertise these gargantuan City claims at all, bearing so little relation as they do to what is ever actually settled, and being liable, moreover, to inflict fresh distress upon the still-traumatised bombing victims, and, for that matter, upon their lawyers.

**Can nothing protect our schools from the forces of darkness?**

Pivoting, then, from one institution of unimpeachable virtue to another. A recent advertisement in this newspaper, placed by Oasis UK, the charity of the Rev Steve Chalke, sought an "accomplished fundraiser" to help realise a vision of "outstanding schools and community hubs" rooted, naturally, in Christian values. The successful applicant, one learns, will be expected to cultivate the "HVD" — the high value dealer, or, in the older idiom, the rich person — and it is notable that neither the fundraiser, nor apparently Mr Chalke himself, is required to have any experience whatsoever of running a school.

But then why should he be? For it has been decided, at the highest level, that the education of our children is a business best entrusted to enthusiastic amateurs: to homophobic religious fanatics, to self-promoting tradesmen, to would-be barons, to parents, to classroom assistants, to anyone, in short, who fancies a go. There is Peter Hyman, Mr Blair's former speechwriter, who went off to Islington Green "to do something different", as though a comprehensive were a sabbatical. There is Reg Vardy, the car salesman, whose sponsorship secured him schools in which creationism might be aired alongside the more conventional heresies. In such capable hands, one asks — and it seems only reasonable to ask — will our children's minds be entirely safe?

**This week**

This week I read Naomi Wolf's *The Treehouse*, in which the celebrated feminist extracts life lessons from her father while helping to build a cabin, and found myself longing to reread *Little House in the Big Woods*, where Pa builds a far better cabin and imparts superior lessons with a fraction of the fuss. This week I also watched, with a shame I can only describe as complete and unrepentant, George Galloway destroying himself, nightly, on *Celebrity Big Brother*. Not in my name, I told myself. But I kept watching.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_2
body:
When *Prospect* magazine went to press, some weeks ago, with its admiring cover profile of the man it called "the last serious politician in Britain", its editors could not have known that by the time subscribers received their copies through the letterbox, the last serious politician in Britain would be presiding over the worst run of by-election results in his party's history, and would have been ridiculed, in the space of a single week, by every sketch writer from Wapping to Kensington. Such are the perils of the long lead time. A monthly magazine, unlike a leader, cannot easily be recalled. And so we have the pleasing spectacle of thousands of copies of a periodical urging us to take seriously a man whom the electorate has, over the intervening fortnight, comprehensively declined to take seriously at all.

To be fair to *Prospect*, whose profile is a masterpiece of the well-meaning, everyone else got it wrong too. When our subject first assumed the leadership, the adjectives came thick and glowing. He was "forensic" (the *Times*). He was "relentless" (the *Guardian*, which meant it as a compliment, once). He was "incisive", possessed of a "surgeon's mind" and, according to one especially breathless sketch, a man who "dismantled his opponents' arguments the way a child dismantles a clock". How we admired the dismantling. How we thrilled to the forensic. It seemed, for a few dizzying months, as though the nation had at last found a leader capable of asking a follow-up question.

The trouble with being forensic, of course, is that the quality does not travel well. Forensic in opposition becomes, in government, merely pedantic; the surgeon's precision, once he is the one being operated upon, looks a good deal like fidgeting. What the press hailed as relentless, the voters of two lost by-elections have decided is simply exhausting. And "incisive" - well, one cuts oneself with an incisive instrument as easily as anyone else. The very traits that made him irresistible to leader-writers have curdled, with the reliability of milk left out in the sun, into precisely the liabilities that now sink him. He cannot stop dismantling. He dismantles his own front bench. He dismantles the goodwill of his colleagues. Given a spare afternoon, one suspects, he would dismantle himself.

**The pattern, for those who have not been paying attention, is a familiar one.** His predecessor was also hailed as a saviour - "the man to detoxify the brand", we were assured, before the brand quietly detoxified him. And the predecessor before that, a figure now so thoroughly forgotten that even the party's own website struggles to spell his name, was likewise greeted, on his election, as the fresh start the party had been praying for. Each was a messiah. Each was abandoned within eighteen months, or, in one memorable case, within eleven. There is by now a settled rhythm to it, a liturgy of acclamation and defenestration so regular you could set a clock by it, assuming there were a child on hand to dismantle it afterwards.

What the pattern most resembles, it seems to me, is the modern relationship between the consumer and the washing machine. One buys the thing in a spasm of optimism, dazzled by the promised cycles, the eco-settings, the reassuring hum of the salesman's patter. For a while it performs admirably. Then, at a point calculated with uncanny precision to fall just outside the warranty, it begins to make an alarming noise, floods the kitchen, and is hauled away to the tip, whereupon one buys another exactly like it and repeats the entire experience. The party has been buying washing machines for a decade now. Each new model is marketed as more efficient, more electable, more forensic than the last. Each expires just past its warranty, which appears, on current evidence, to be shrinking. The next leader will get a year. The one after that, one begins to suspect, a long weekend.

And here one begins to worry, as good citizens must, about the environmental cost. What becomes of all these discarded leaders? They do not, mercifully, decompose - a former party leader has a half-life comparable to polystyrene - but nor do they entirely disappear. They accumulate. They pile up in the after-dinner speaking circuit, in the non-executive directorships, in the memoirs nobody reads, a great landfill of abandoned saviours leaching their bitterness slowly into the political water table. Somewhere there ought to be a recycling scheme, a bring-bank into which spent leaders could be deposited on a Monday morning alongside the Chardonnay bottles. Until then we must simply accept that the party's approach to leadership, like its approach to everything else, is neither reduce, reuse, nor recycle, but merely: buy another, and pray it lasts past the by-election. It never does.

**The last of the great school-bred radicals**

The obituaries of the veteran left-wing journalist who died last week dwelt, as such obituaries always do, on his lifelong hatred of privilege, his unwavering solidarity with the downtrodden, and his education at one of the more brutal of our great public schools, where, we are told, the house system reduced him to years of misery. Nobody has thought to connect these facts, but I shall. The British left, I am increasingly persuaded, is the direct and unintended product of the English house system. Deprive a sensitive boy of hot water, subject him to institutionalised cruelty administered by prefects, and you produce, with the reliability of a production line, a lifelong opponent of the established order. Soften the schools - abolish the cold showers, humanise the housemasters, install pastoral care and mindfulness sessions - and you risk, within a generation, running clean out of revolutionaries.

Consider the case of a certain contemporary musician, much celebrated for the anti-establishment fury of his lyrics, who was, it emerges, formed entirely by a moderately strict grammar school and its rugby master. Remove the rugby master and you remove the fury. The irony, then, is exquisite. The government minister currently campaigning to restore competitive sport to our schools - all that character-building humiliation on the touchline - imagines he is manufacturing patriots. In fact, by this iron logic, he is breeding the next generation of firebrands for the far left. One can only wish him every success.

**On my catastrophic failure to integrate**

A new report informs me that the well-adjusted citizen ought to maintain friendships across the racial divide, and, auditing my own circle in a spirit of penitence, I find myself woefully deficient - not merely in racial diversity, but in every conceivable category. I have no Welsh friends. I have no estate-agent friends. I have no baronet friends, no friends who breed pigs, no friends who have been to space. My social circle, subjected to the report's methodology, is revealed as a monoculture of alarming completeness. The question the report neglects to answer is how, precisely, one is supposed to remedy this in adult life. Is there to be friendship leave, a fortnight's paid absence from work in order to acquire a baronet? A national lottery, perhaps, in which each citizen is randomly assigned twenty new friends from across the demographic spectrum, delivered fortnightly like a vegetable box? One trembles only for the unlucky soul who opens the box to find he has drawn the last serious politician in Britain.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_5
body:
When Susie Orbach launched her Antidote campaign a few years ago, aiming to bring something she called "emotional literacy" into British public life, the response of the cynic — and I confess to being one — was a snigger. Emotions? In public life? Amid the massed ranks of tax-avoiding peers and briefing-crazed spin doctors? What next: mindfulness for the whips' office, active listening at Prime Minister's Questions? And yet, before we dismiss the whole thing as another well-meaning wheeze of the therapeutic classes, we should note that Tessa Jowell was a signatory. And we should further note, with the dawning discomfort of one who has laughed too soon, that the campaign appears to have triumphed beyond its founders' wildest hopes. Our politicians are now fluent, indeed positively garrulous, in the language of the marriage-guidance counsellor.

Consider the scene last week, when the prime minister, having received an apology of some description from some quarter or other, announced with the beatific calm of a man three sessions into couples' work that this would allow "everyone to draw a line and move on". Draw a line. Move on. He was not, you understand, expressing an opinion; he was facilitating a process. Within hours his press secretary was assuring the lobby that it was now time for "closure", and by the afternoon the culture secretary — never one to be left off a hymn sheet — had been heard hoping that "the whole country" could now "move on together". One almost expected them to arrange the chairs in a circle, dim the lights, and pass round a box of tissues. Here, plainly, was the Antidote's dream made flesh: an entire cabinet emotionally literate, running a group therapy session for the nation, gently but firmly nudging us all towards healing.

The trouble, of course, is that this vocabulary is not emotional honesty at all. It is a weapon. "Move on" and "closure", in the mouth of a minister, mean precisely one thing, and it is not what Susie Orbach had in mind. They mean: I have won, so bugger off.

Were I to lie down on the prime minister's couch and describe my feelings, I would report that I feel, on the contrary, rather stuck. I feel betrayed. I find it difficult to trust. These, I am given to understand, are perfectly valid feelings, and I should not be shamed out of them merely because the person who has wronged me would very much prefer that I stopped having them. Indeed, I have been consulting the literature. There is an admirable self-help volume — I recommend it to any distressed citizen — called *Let It Go: The Healing Power of Forgiveness*, by the American therapist Dr Marjorie Fenn, which lays out the whole business in reassuring numbered steps. And here is the first thing Dr Fenn wants me to understand: the person who hurt you has already moved on. They are getting on with their life. They are not lying awake. This rings devastatingly true, for the prime minister, having drawn his line, is already busily launching his next inquiry, having apparently drawn a line under the findings of the last one before the ink was dry.

Dr Fenn's second strategy is more demanding. In order to forgive, she suggests, one should sit quietly and recall the good times shared with the person who hurt you — the warmth, the promises, the sense that here was someone you could depend upon. I have tried this. I recalled the pledge that there would be no rise in tuition fees, the assurance that the dossier was beyond doubt, the vow that this was a listening government, the solemn undertaking that we would be told the truth. Such good times! Such balmy afternoons of trust! I found, dwelling on them, that far from being nudged towards forgiveness I was seized by a strong desire to write a stiff letter, but perhaps that is my own emotional immaturity showing.

What genuinely puzzles the citizen-patient is why politicians should reach for the counselling lexicon at all. The actual practitioners of the art — the people who sit in the beige rooms and charge by the hour — are notably reluctant to use these phrases in the confident, declarative manner favoured at Westminster. To a real therapist, "moving on" is a laborious, halting, frequently reversed process, undertaken by the injured party at their own pace, and never, ever imposed by the person who did the injuring. "Closure" is perhaps the most abject and threadbare of all counselling clichés, and even those who deploy it agree on one point: you cannot claim it on somebody else's behalf. You cannot stride into a room, announce that everyone now has closure, and stride out again. That is not healing. That is merely winning, with a scented candle.

**The overseer of the overseers**

Which brings us, by way of the smoothest possible transition, to the culture secretary and her single, sovereign, all-purpose reassurance. Whenever anyone raises the faintest doubt about the integrity of a certain forthcoming appointment — let us say, for the sake of argument, the chairmanship of the BBC — the culture secretary has an answer, and it is always the same answer. The process, she explains, is being scrupulously overseen by a distinguished independent figure whose probity is beyond question. Who selected the shortlist? A matter for the distinguished figure. Was the outcome pre-cooked? Entirely a matter for the distinguished figure. And who, one longs to ask, oversees the distinguished figure? Here the culture secretary smiles the smile of a woman who has drawn a line, and moves on.

It seems worth pausing on this paragon, this guarantor of national fairness. His curriculum vitae, which I have been unable to read in a single sitting, discloses that he is at various times chairman, deputy chairman and non-executive director of a great many boards; a visiting professor of leadership at two universities and an honorary professor of leadership at a third; a senior consulting fellow to a think tank; a strategic adviser to a consultancy that advises other consultancies; and the recipient of what the trade press coyly calls "a portfolio of remunerated positions". He is, in the truest sense, a busy man, and it is a wonder he can spare the afternoon to guarantee our fairness at all.

His philosophy of leadership, happily, has been committed to print, and it repays study. A true leader, he writes, must know when to soar "at thirty thousand feet", surveying the whole landscape — but must also know the moment "to land the aircraft and walk". He offers a list of the things employees are silently, secretly asking of their bosses, among them: "Acknowledge the greatness within me." He has composed a poem — I have seen it, reproduced on the endpapers of one of his volumes — comparing the life of the modern executive to a circus, complete with high wire, clowns and the ever-present possibility of falling. And he has helpfully distilled the leadership principles of a celebrated American general into a numbered creed, of which my favourite runs: "Don't let adverse facts stand in the way of a good decision." It is, I think we can agree, exactly the maxim one wants engraved above the door of the man appointed to guarantee that no adverse fact has stood in the way of the decision about who shall run the BBC.

Whether any of this qualifies him for the role, or whether it ought to qualify him instead for a long and restful sabbatical at thirty thousand feet, I could not possibly say. And should you press me, I would only be able to do what the culture secretary does, and refer you to the distinguished figure himself. His suitability, his methods, his poems, his soaring and his landing and his walking — all of it, she would gently remind us, drawing a line and moving serenely on, is entirely a matter for the overseer.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_6
body:
**Come and have a big, honest, national conversation with us. No, really**

Cynics, we were warned, would sneer. "There will be those," the leader announced, forehead creased into the practised grooves of pre-emptive martyrdom, "who dismiss this as a gimmick, who scoff at the very idea that a government might want to listen. To them I say: judge us by what we do." A safe enough challenge, since the doing, in this case, consists entirely of a website; and a website, moreover, whose contents are so uniformly, so radiantly appreciative that judging the government by them is rather like judging a monarch by the applause of his own courtiers.

For the Big Dialogue has now been running for some weeks, and any citizen curious to eavesdrop on the honest, grown-up exchange promised at its launch will find, instead, a spectacle of gratitude so relentless that it begins, after a page or two, to induce a mild vertigo. "I just want to say thank you," writes Mrs D Perkins of Rugby, "for the prosperity we now enjoy. My family have never had it so good, and we all know who to thank." Mr T Sedgwick of Bolton is of one mind with her: "When I think back to the misery of the last lot, the queues, the despair, the sense that nothing worked - and then I look at Britain today - I could weep with relief." A Mr H Chaudhry, likewise, "cannot imagine what our lives would be like had the other party remained in power. Dark days indeed."

The unanimity is, frankly, uncanny. One begins to suspect either a remarkable coincidence of feeling or a remarkably diligent moderator. Every so often a contributor essays what the website's own guidance notes term "constructive criticism," and here the effect is more comic still, for the criticism turns out to be so bland, so exquisitely deferential, as to constitute a further species of praise. "If I had one small suggestion," ventures Mrs L Openshaw, "it would be that the government's achievements are not always trumpeted loudly enough. We should be prouder of what we have done." Even the complaints, in other words, complain that the compliments are insufficient.

Nestled among these effusions, I came across one submission of arresting eloquence, which I reproduce in full: "It was a bright cold day in April, and the clocks were striking thirteen... the whole climate of thought will be different. In fact there will be no thought, as we understand it now. Orthodoxy means not thinking - not needing to think. Orthodoxy is unconsciousness." I could find no attribution, but the sentiment fitted so snugly among its neighbours that no moderator, evidently, had thought to remove it. One notes only that the fellow who wrote it originally did so about a party rather keen on national conversations of its own.

What is most striking, however, is not what the Big Dialogue contains but what it does not. Here is a country at war, or so we are told; a country beset, we are also told, by terrorism, by crumbling hospitals, by a constitution held together with sticky tape and ministerial improvisation. And yet, in all the thousands of grateful words, not one contributor appears to have noticed any of it. No one asks about the soldiers. No one asks about the surveillance, the detention without trial, the schools without teachers. The only grievances permitted through the filter are of two carefully calibrated kinds. There is the hopelessly parochial personal petition - Mr G Naylor of Stoke would like "something done about the potholes on Acacia Road, which have been a menace for years" - and there is the grievance helpfully redirected at the public itself. "I do wish," writes Mrs P Ellison, "that people would take more responsibility for their own litter, rather than always blaming the council." Quite so. The government, it emerges, is faultless; the potholes are geological; and any remaining unpleasantness is the fault of the citizen, who really ought to try harder.

Stalin, one recalls, had his ministers construct entire villages of painted façade along the routes his inspectors travelled, so that they might gaze out upon a contented and prosperous peasantry that did not, in any meaningful sense, exist. The Big Dialogue is a Potemkin village rendered in pixels: a long, sunlit avenue of happy frontages, behind which there is nothing at all. Turning its pages, one is put irresistibly in mind of a pre-1989 edition of *Pravda*, in which the harvest was always a record, the workers always jubilant, and the leadership always, mysteriously, both humble and infallible at once.

Whether this is the product of active censorship or merely of native apathy, I confess I cannot say. Perhaps somewhere a diligent official is quietly deleting every reference to war, terror and the NHS, leaving only the potholes and the thank-yous. Or perhaps - and this is the more dispiriting possibility - the public really did submit nothing else; perhaps, offered the chance to say anything at all to their rulers, the citizenry chose overwhelmingly to say "well done." One hardly knows which explanation to prefer. What one does know is that an initiative christened the Big Dialogue, launched by a government warning us against cynicism and populated by choruses of orchestrated adoration, has managed to summon, from the collective memory, the one two-word phrase its authors must least have wished to evoke. Big Brother, it turns out, was watching the launch. The cynics, as promised, are having their field day.

**Sorry, but which bin does an MBE go in?**

Meanwhile, in the parallel theatre of conscience, the columnist and public intellectual Professor Julian Frayne has announced, at considerable length and across two broadsheet pages, that he will be returning his MBE. This raises, at once, a practical difficulty that no one seems to have addressed: where, precisely, does one return an honour to? I picture a municipal facility on the edge of town, between the tip and the garden centre - the Civic Honours Recycling Depot - with clearly labelled skips for medals, ribbons and scrolls, a helpful attendant in a hi-vis tabard, and a sign reminding conscience-stricken citizens to remove all sentimental packaging before deposit. Returned honours to be sorted, presumably, and re-issued to the next deserving nobody.

Professor Frayne is at pains to explain that he intends no disrespect to those who still, touchingly, value such baubles. "For the little people," he writes - and the phrase is his, not mine - "the nurses, the dinner ladies, the community stalwarts, an honour still means a great deal, and rightly so." One is reminded of Leona Helmsley's observation that only little people pay taxes. The little people, it seems, may keep their gongs; it is the large people, the people with columns, who must nobly renounce them. Benjamin Zephaniah, at least, had the grace to decline his outright, rather than accept it, cherish it, and then extract a second helping of publicity by handing it back.

I have a modest proposal. Since the honours lists already offer ordinary people their fleeting sensation of importance, let each be henceforth accompanied by a supplementary Returned Honours List - name, achievement, and principled reason for refusal - so that the dinner ladies and community stalwarts, too, might briefly experience what it is to matter enormously. It would cost nothing, and would grant to the little people, for one glorious morning, precisely the feeling the columnists already enjoy every week, for money, on the page.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/validation/0/validation_2
body:
Is the glass half-full or half-empty, prime minister? You taught us the trick yourself, and now it seems only mannerly to return it, in this the hour of the great Channel 3 licensing débâcle, when the two possible outcomes are so agreeably repellent that the phrase "worst of both worlds" seems, for once, to fall short. In one corner we have a pompous impresario so devoted to the promotion of his own brand — his own face, his own name, his own signature squiggle on every letterhead — that one imagines him having it tattooed, quietly, in a discreet place, so as to remind himself, first thing in the morning, exactly whom he is dealing with. In the other, a corporate operator of such proven, industrial-strength avarice that even in a nation not much given to fellow-feeling it has achieved the near-impossible: universal loathing, across all classes, regions and demographics. Half-empty, then. And also, if I may, half-empty.

There is, I concede, a dark comedy in the spectacle of the loser weeping. On the courthouse steps, the vanquished bidder — I will not say which, since both wept, though only one wept on camera — dabbed at reddened eyes and spoke in the cracked, catch-in-the-throat register you yourself, prime minister, have perfected for occasions of national tragedy and the smiles on sick kiddies' faces. Little Nell herself expired with less fuss.

**Once, we denounced them**

Cast your mind back, prime minister, to the sunlit uplands of opposition, when you and your colleagues were free to be as righteous as you pleased. You will remember the profit-making operator as it appeared then: a rapacious, gouging middleman, siphoning public money into private pockets, offering the nation nothing in return but repeats and a hotline. Your culture spokesman — now, of course, your culture secretary — pledged, with that special quiver of moral certainty, to "replace the profiteers with a model that serves the people, not the shareholders". A non-profit trust, he said. Like they have in other, better-run countries. Like they have, he specified, in one of those admirable Scandinavian democracies where everything works and nobody weeps on courthouse steps. "The people," he said, "deserve better than to have their airwaves auctioned to the highest and greediest bidder." Hear, hear, we cried, from our sofas.

And now the people have got — well. The people have got a commission.

**Not unlike the Dome**

I know you dislike the comparison, prime minister, but it must be made: this is your Dome. It has the same essential architecture of catastrophe — the grand promise, the bungled delivery, the sense that no living soul is actually steering the thing — and in one crucial respect it is worse. You never, in opposition, denounced the Dome. You inherited the Dome; you could plausibly plead that it was already rolling downhill when you arrived. But the profiteers you denounced with your own lips, and having denounced them you have now contrived a process so incompetent that they may end up running the whole enterprise after all, richer and more insufferable than before. The one uniting feature of both fiascos — the sole thread of continuity in an otherwise seamless tapestry of failure — is that no one of any political consequence will be held to account for either. Both are being presented to us, in the soothing tones of the departmental briefing, as natural phenomena. Weather. Tectonics. Forces too vast and complex for mere ministers to master. It rained on the Dome; the market blew through the licence. What can one poor culture secretary do against the tides?

**The commission of all the talents**

One might ask — I do ask — why the responsible minister assembled a commission so conspicuously innocent of relevant expertise. Here was a panel convened to adjudicate the most consequential broadcasting decision of the decade, and it contained, so far as anyone could discover, not a single person who had ever run a television channel, sold advertising, or knowingly watched daytime programming. The chair, in particular, remains a mystery of appointment on the scale of the Turin Shroud: a genial figure of no discernible qualification, plucked from what obscurity we may only guess at, and installed to weigh bids he was manifestly unequipped to read. And read them he did — both of them, for there were only two — before dismissing both as inadequate, and then, having dismissed both, granting one of them, and one only, a second go. To the impresario, naturally. Not to the profiteers. A piece of open, blushless favouritism so flagrant that it would have most toddlers reeling with sympathetic indignation; nursery school being, after all, the one institution in this country that takes fairness seriously.

**The lawyers, at least, are on the case**

The spurned profiteers, being profiteers, did what profiteers do: they went to law. And they won. The judge, whose language I commend to your speechwriters as a model of clarity, found the commission's conduct "conspicuously unfair", the decision "unlawful", the whole business "an abuse of power". One might have imagined that a chair so comprehensively rebuked would resign before nightfall, perhaps before the ink dried. He did not. He was, initially, supported by the minister — publicly, warmly, in that special departmental way that means the resignation letter is already in the second drawer. When at last he went, grudgingly, weeks later, it was attributed not to his own conduct, ruled unlawful by a High Court judge, but to "media vilification"; the media, as ever, having behaved outrageously in reporting what had happened. His fellow commissioners, meanwhile, elected to soldier on under a fresh chair, a successor who has candidly acknowledged that the entire body is now discredited, while insisting, in the same breath, that there is simply no time to replace it. No time! The whole point of a discredited body, apparently, is that one must keep it, urgently, because replacing it would take too long.

So the thing has been settled, in the end, by lawyers. It could hardly have been otherwise. The losing impresario has a long and lovingly tended appetite for litigation; he sues the way other men do the crossword. And around him has gathered a whole guild of the learned: the treasury solicitors who blessed the original unfair decision, the corporate QC who dismantled it, the eminent City firm now retained to oversee the final deliberations — a firm which, by a coincidence too delicious to invent, has at one time or another acted for every interested party in the affair. There is no one left in the room who is not being paid by the hour to be there.

**Let the lawyers run everything**

Which brings me, prime minister, to my modest proposal. Given the choice between the political appointees, who could not organise a two-horse race without disqualifying both horses and then re-entering one, and the lawyers, who are demonstrably brainy, decisive, and available, why do we persist with the fiction of democratic government at all? Dispense with the incompetent placemen. Dispense, if it comes to that, with the commission, the minister, the whole apparatus of accountable-in-theory-unaccountable-in-fact administration. Hand the country to the QCs. They already run this saga; let them run the rest. At least they turn up on time and read the papers.

And here, prime minister, is the final, exquisite twist. You are already doing it. Peer by peer, silk by silk, you have been quietly staffing your government with lords and lawyers, appointing your favourite advocates to the great offices, ennobling the learned friends, converting the cabinet into something very like a well-upholstered set of chambers. You have simply not finished yet. Give it time. Give it, as the new commission chair might say, no time at all — but urgently.

*Related: "Why weeping in public is the new statesmanship" · "Ten things you never knew about the man who signs everything" · A spokesman for the commission said last night that lessons had been learned and that the process had "achieved what it set out to do".*

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_103
body:
Somewhere between eight and ten million people have died. The killing has continued, on and off, for the best part of a decade. It is the deadliest conflict since the second world war. And this week, our newspapers have been telling us about the Home Secretary's love affair.

I don't wish to belittle the political and constitutional questions raised by a minister's private life. But there is a small matter of perspective. While the front pages have been convulsed by adultery, war has resumed in the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo. The Rwandan army has crossed back into the north-eastern DRC. Rival factions of the Congolese army, some loyal to Rwanda, are fighting each other. Civilians are being killed, raped, tortured and driven from their homes. Even before this latest outbreak, according to the International Rescue Committee, more than 1,000 people were dying every day, from the disease and malnutrition caused by the previous phase of the conflict. Almost half of them were under five. In total, this war has already killed some 3.8 million people. And we are worrying about a Home Secretary's affair.

There was a time when Rwanda's involvement in the Congo could be defended. When the Interahamwe militias, which slaughtered 800,000 people in the genocide of 1994, fled across the border into what was then Zaire, they were sheltered first by Mobutu and then by Laurent Kabila. They threatened to reinvade Rwanda. Kagame's government had every reason to pursue them. But after the second invasion, in 1998, something changed. Rwanda abandoned the hunt for the genocidaires and turned instead to the exploitation of the Congo's minerals: its diamonds and its coltan. By 1999, according to a UN security council report, some 80% of Rwanda's military budget - around $320m a year - was being financed by stolen Congolese minerals.

This was part of a six-nation turf war over the mines of the eastern Congo. Millions of people were driven from their homes; thousands were forced to work in the mines or into prostitution. But of all the armies operating there, the Rwandan operation was the most efficient. It was, Amnesty International records, controlled directly from Kigali. And it continued even after Rwanda's official withdrawal in 2002. The current invasion is a thinly disguised attempt to neutralise the militias which now threaten Rwanda's mineral business.

So why do we hear so little about it? We wept for Rwanda in 1994, and rightly so. We wring our hands over Darfur. But the Congo, whose death toll dwarfs them both, scarcely registers. The reason, I think, is that it does not fit the story we have chosen to tell. We are accustomed to seeing the Rwandan forces as the good guys: the survivors of genocide, the men who drove out the killers. It is psychologically almost impossible to accept that the victims of one of the century's greatest crimes could become the perpetrators of another. But this is precisely what has happened. Some of the bloodiest war criminals now at large are the men we have canonised.

I anticipate the objection: that the case against Kagame's government has been assembled by a UN security council pursuing its own power games. It hasn't. The same conclusions have been reached, independently, by Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch, the International Crisis Group, Global Witness, the British all-party parliamentary group on the Great Lakes, and even the US state department. These are not bodies with an axe to grind against Rwanda. They have simply looked at the evidence.

And the evidence is overwhelming. It fills hundreds of pages: eyewitness accounts, testimony from survivors and from perpetrators. It records the rape of children as young as three; the genital mutilation of women who resisted; forced labour in the mines, where those who could no longer work were buried alive; the torching of villages, the looting, the killing of those who resisted, and the flight of millions into the jungle, where most of the 3.8 million deaths took place, from hunger and disease. The marauding armies did not shoot most of these people. But they bear an equivalent responsibility for their deaths. The documents name the agents and the victims. They give dates and locations. They record the value of the stolen resources and the names of the buyers.

There are those - the journalist Victoria Brittain among them - who argue that the critics have confused Rwanda's disciplined national army with the chaotic rebel groups it is alleged to control. This will not wash. The US state department records "numerous credible reports" of regular Rwandan troops killing, torturing and raping in Kivu and Maniema. But even if it were true, it would make no moral difference. There is no distinction to be drawn between killing directly and commissioning others to kill on your behalf. Rwanda created the RCD-Goma militia. It was formed in Kigali. It was financed and equipped by Rwanda, according to Global Witness and Amnesty International, with heavy weapons. To hide behind your proxies is not to be exonerated by them.

Yes, the government in Kinshasa has also committed crimes. But its authority barely runs in the east. A UN report leaked to the BBC confirms that Rwanda and its proxies are the dominant forces in the eastern DRC, that they control most of the minerals, and that they are involved in almost all the fighting there. And what of the Interahamwe, the excuse for all this? Rwanda could have destroyed them - now much weakened - years ago. The International Crisis Group points out that Rwanda enjoyed total military control of the eastern Congo between 1996 and 2002, and still failed to neutralise them. The British parliamentary group found that Rwandan forces were frequently stationed where no Interahamwe existed, and sometimes even allied with them, the better to control the mines. Rwanda is not fighting the genocidaires. It is using them as a pretext to drag the eastern Congo back into war.

The international community could stop this tomorrow. Rwanda is a tiny, fragile state, dependent on foreign aid, more than a third of which comes from Britain. A word from us would carry weight. But nothing will change until the public wakes up to this war, and abandons the comforting fiction that the victims of atrocious crimes cannot themselves become the perpetrators of them.

www.monbiot.com

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_104
body:
The claim now surfaces everywhere. Barbara Probst Solomon detects the emergence of a "soft fascism" in the United States. Richard Sennett has warned of something similar. Across the internet, the accusation multiplies like bacteria in a warm dish: George W Bush's America, we are told, is either fascist already or on the very cusp of becoming so. It is a comforting diagnosis, for it summons the memory of a defeated enemy, and it lets us believe that we know how the story ends. But it is wrong, and its wrongness matters, for a false diagnosis prescribes the wrong cure.

Robert Paxton, in The Anatomy of Fascism, offers as precise a definition as we are likely to get. Fascism, he argues, is a politics of communal decline, of humiliation and victimhood, in which a nation persuades itself that it has been betrayed and defiled, and seeks redemption in compensatory cults of unity, energy and purity. It was allied to the elite, certainly, but it did not begin there. It arose from below, from the hysterical mobilisation of the masses, from a popular frenzy which the old guard first feared, then rode. Look at Bush and you will find none of this. He does not summon a delirious multitude into the streets. He provokes no ecstasy of communal humiliation, no cult of the redemptive nation reborn through struggle. His is not a politics of frenzy but of complacency. The people he serves feel not humiliated but blessed.

So the framing collapses. But this does not mean that Bush's project is unprecedented, or that it has emerged from nowhere. It means that it repeats a different ideology altogether, one we have failed to recognise because we have been squinting in the wrong direction. To defeat it, we must first identify it.

Puritanism is the most misunderstood force in our history. We imagine it as a joyless cult of self-denial: the stripping of churches, the banning of maypoles and playhouses, the grey suit and the pinched face. And this seems remote indeed from the conspicuous gluttony of Republican America, its stretch limousines and mega-churches and its worship of wealth. But Puritanism was never chiefly a matter of self-denial. It was the product of an economic transformation, and its true meaning lies there.

In the England of the early 17th century, the remnants of the feudal state performed a function not unlike that of 20th-century social democracy. It served the monarchy and the clergy, but it also regulated economic exploitation. As R H Tawney showed in Religion and the Rise of Capitalism (1926), Charles I attempted to nationalise industries, to control foreign exchange, and to prosecute the lords who evicted their peasants, the employers who underpaid their workers and the magistrates who denied the poor their relief. The state, in other words, still held that the strong owed obligations to the weak.

This model could not survive, and it did not. Over 150 years, the great international commercial companies concentrated financial power on a scale never before seen, subjecting the old medieval organisation of industry to a new money-power. It was commerce and finance, not industry, that drove the transformation. Monarchs became the puppets of financiers. In England, the dissolution of the monasteries loosed a colossal seizure of wealth by a new commercial class: the enclosure of land, the eviction of the multitude, the speculation in estates, and the invention of sophisticated financial markets experimenting in futures and arbitrage - all the vices we now associate with the Age of Enron.

The early Reformation theologians denounced this exploitation with fury. They preached charity and justice, and demanded that the exploiter be punished. This spirit crossed the Atlantic with the New England settlers and survived among them; in England it was crushed. Puritanism became the religion of the new commercial classes: the traders, the money-lenders, the bankers and the industrialists. What Calvin gave them the old order never could: a theological justification of commerce. Capitalism, at last, could glorify God.

Trace the logic and you can see how the world was turned inside out. From Calvin's doctrine of individual purification came an idealisation of personal responsibility before God, which became, in time, a theory of individual rights, reversing the traditional scheme of Christian virtues. By the middle of the 17th century, most English Puritans regarded poverty not as misfortune but as moral failure, and wealth not as a peril to the soul but as a blessing rewarding energy and will. Individualism in religion produced an individualist morality, and individualist morality produced the disparagement of the social fabric. Then the Puritans performed their masterstroke: they conflated the religious calling with the commercial one. The preacher Richard Steele, in 1684, could write of the tradesman serving God in his calling. Business success became proof of spiritual grace. Joseph Lee, anticipating Adam Smith's invisible hand by more than a century, could claim that the advancement of private persons served the public good - which meant, in practice, that men of property might destroy everyone else's advancement while congratulating themselves on their piety.

Tawney characterised the Puritans as early converts to what he called "administrative nihilism": the doctrine of the minimal state. Business affairs, they held, should be settled by businessmen, unhampered by the antiquated morality of the medieval church. They formulated a new theory of social obligation, in which helping the poor was not charity but corruption, breeding idleness and spiritual dissolution. There is one difference between them and Bush's people, and only one: the Puritans worshipped production, where our contemporaries worship consumption. But this is the same disease presenting a different symptom. The world, Tawney wrote, exists not to be enjoyed but to be conquered. Only its conqueror deserves the name of Christian.

Those who remained true to the original spirit of the Reformation - the Levellers, the Diggers - were violently suppressed. And the pursuit of adulterers and sodomites served, then as now, as a useful distraction for the impoverished lower classes, who might otherwise have asked awkward questions about who had taken their land. Read Ronan Bennett's novel Havoc in its Third Year, set in the 1630s, and you read a parable of our own age: the obsession with terrorists (Irish and Jesuit), the persecution of homosexuality, the panic about sexual licence, the vicious punishment of moral deviance, the disparagement of public support for the poor. Swap the black suits for grey and the characters walk straight out of Bush's America.

Why has this ideology returned in 2004? Because it must. The enrichment of the elite and the impoverishment of the lower classes cannot be sustained without an ideology to justify them, and in the United States that ideology must be religious. Bush's government is driven back to Puritanism not by whim but by historical necessity. To understand what we are confronting, we must look not to the 1930s, but to the 1630s.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_105
body:
There was a wise man in Vienna,
Whose task was, in effect, a chimera:
    To take bombs away
    While he gave, the same day,
The means to make more. What a career-a.

Spare a thought for Mohamed El Baradei. The head of the International Atomic Energy Agency is required to do two things at once, and they cannot both be done. With one hand he must strip the world's nations of their capacity to build nuclear weapons. With the other he must distribute to them the materials, the technology and the expertise required to build precisely those weapons. His predecessor, Hans Blix, laboured under the same impossible mandate. It is a contradiction so fundamental that it should long ago have collapsed the agency into absurdity. Instead it has merely allowed Iran, a fortnight ago, to wave away the agency's demands with a shrug: everything we are doing, the Iranians correctly observe, is permitted by the treaties we have signed.

The absurdity has a distinguished lineage. It begins in December 1953, when President Eisenhower stood before the general assembly of the United Nations and delivered the speech we now remember as "Atoms for Peace". It was a moment of extraordinary optimism, and it is worth recalling just how giddy that optimism was. A newspaper of the period promised its readers that atomic energy would soon power their motorcars, abolish the misery of the coal mines and usher in what one editorial called, without embarrassment, "an earthly paradise". Eisenhower spoke of taking the nuclear sword out of the hands of soldiers and placing it, as a ploughshare, in the hands of engineers. The atom, he declared, would be applied "to the needs of agriculture, medicine and other peaceful activities". Fissionable material would be distributed to the "power-starved" regions of the world, and this bounty would serve as an incentive: the recipients would forswear weapons in exchange for the blessings of civilian power.

Out of this speech came the IAEA, established in 1957, and, in time, the Non-Proliferation Treaty. Both documents encode the same two purposes, welded uneasily together. The nations without the bomb must never acquire it; the nations with the technology must share it. Prevent weapons; spread technology. The treaty is, in effect, a bargain struck between two irreconcilable clauses of itself.

The bargain made a kind of sense only while the dream remained dreamable. But the dream did not survive contact with the physics. Almost every state that has developed nuclear weapons over the past 30 years has done so by quietly diverting the resources of a civilian nuclear programme, and in several cases with the direct assistance of Atoms for Peace and its successors. Israel, South Africa, India, Pakistan, North Korea, Iraq: in each case the road to the bomb ran through the reactor. India built its device with help supplied, at various times, by Canada, the United States, Germany, France, Norway and the United Kingdom. Pakistan, not to be outdone, received parallel largesse. We equipped these nations for total war in the name of peace, and called it non-proliferation. Some 20 countries could now, thanks to foreign civilian assistance, become weapons states within a matter of months. And Russia, let us be clear, was not merely permitted but obliged under the terms of the treaty to ship uranium and bomb-making technology to Iran. This is not a loophole in the system. It is the system.

Consider what Iran is actually doing. It is enriching uranium and producing heavy water. Both processes are entirely legitimate for the generation of electricity. Both are also, with only modest adjustment, the means of building a bomb. To enrich uranium to bomb-grade requires nothing more than additional passes through the centrifuges that already exist. The NPT hands Iran the materials and the legal cover simultaneously. If you want a nuclear weapon, the procedure is now well established: you sign the treaties, you join the IAEA, you accept the inspectors, and you exercise your entitlements. The treaty is not the obstacle to a weapons programme. It is the blueprint.

And why might Iran want the bomb? Israel possesses a substantial nuclear arsenal, has never signed the NPT, and is protected in its refusal by the United States. The US and the UK have abandoned their own disarmament obligations under the treaty and have both mused publicly about the pre-emptive use of nuclear weapons. American military bases encircle Iran on almost every side. And the lesson of North Korea is impossible to miss: acquire the bomb and you acquire impunity, a seat at the negotiating table and a guarantee against invasion. If Iran is not seeking nuclear weapons, then it has profoundly misread the world in which it lives.

Into this Mr El Baradei walks, armed with nothing but a plea. He can only beg Iran to accept "voluntary agreements", because the treaty he is charged with enforcing gives him no authority to stop enrichment that the treaty itself permits. This is the true measure of his impotence. And the danger it exposes is not confined to one country. The official nuclear powers have effectively junked the NPT, while the non-nuclear powers have learned to exploit it. The result could be a nuclear cascade across the Middle East: Hezbollah striking Israel, Israel turning upon Iran, and a second nuclear arms race dragging in everyone within reach of the fallout.

The conclusion is obvious, though almost no one is prepared to say it out loud. You cannot phase out nuclear weapons while you continue to phase in nuclear power. The two are the same technology at different stages of the same road, and every reactor is a bomb factory awaiting a decision. We need a new nuclear treaty, one founded on the grim realities of the world as it is, rather than on Eisenhower's earthly paradise. Until we have it, the head of the IAEA will continue in his appointed task: to save the world by supplying it with the means of its own destruction.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_106
body:
Why does a country stuffed with brilliant people so often find itself choosing between two profoundly unimpressive men? The answer is simple, and unkind: because unimpressive men keep getting elected. And so it is this year. As the presidential race gathers its dreary momentum, the progressive establishment has once again issued its instructions to the faithful. Choose the safe one. Choose the dull one. Choose John Kerry.

Let me lay out their case with the sympathy it deserves, for it is not a stupid one. Almost every progressive voice worth listening to has now urged swing-state voters to back the Democrat. The Guardian has said it. The Nation has said it. Even Noam Chomsky has said it. Their logic runs as follows: Kerry is uninspiring — about as inspiring as a parking lot on a wet Sunday in Detroit — but he is the only man who can remove George Bush from office, and the race is so close that a few thousand votes cast for Ralph Nader in the wrong states could hand the presidency back to the incumbent. The Republicans understand this perfectly, which is why they have been quietly funnelling money into Nader's campaign. The argument, in other words, has real merit. I concede it at once.

And I concede, too, that the world would be safer without Bush. This is a president who has launched two unnecessary wars, threatened dozens of other nations, torn up international treaties and domestic regulations with equal relish, handed corporations a licence to accelerate the destruction of the climate, presided over a global assault on civil liberties, and set about dismantling the very idea that the state should tax the rich in order to help the poor. There is no exaggeration required here. He is a catastrophe.

So the progressive papers make what they call the "exceptional circumstances" argument. A Guardian leader has told us that whatever Kerry's flaws, they are overshadowed by the neoconservative agenda and the catastrophic war-making of the man he seeks to replace. The Nation's open letter to Nader insists that "2004 is not 2000," and that Bush's defeat is critical.

There is only one problem with this. Both publications said precisely the same thing in 2000. The Nation's Eric Alterman blamed Al Gore's defeat on Nader's "megalomania." The Guardian, just before that election, called Gore "the safe, wise choice," while conceding he was neither exciting nor visionary. And if you go back further, and forward, you will find the same warning issued in nearly every modern presidential contest. There are always exceptional circumstances. There will always be exceptional circumstances. This is not an accident. It is the necessary product of a political system built on patronage, corruption and the manufacture of fear, a system that cannot help but produce dreadful candidates, and which therefore cannot help but generate the emergency that justifies voting for them. Only a leader prepared to overturn that system and reintroduce democracy will ever break the cycle. Vote for the bad to stop the terrible in 2004, and you guarantee the identical dilemma in 2008, and in 2012, and onward, indefinitely, until the sun burns out.

Breaking the cycle requires courage of a particular kind: the willingness to take on the corporations that buy elections and the media that police the boundaries of what may be discussed. And here we arrive at the heart of the matter. John Kerry, it is true, showed genuine courage in Vietnam. He showed it again when he came home and told the truth about that war. But on the campaign trail he displays no courage whatever. He has said that "even knowing what we now know" he would still have voted to authorise the invasion of Iraq. His national security adviser, James Rubin, told the Washington Post that Kerry would "in all probability" have attacked Iraq by now had he been president. His much-celebrated fundraising is not a triumph of democracy but a record of corporate investment: his backers fund him because they expect compliance, and they are unlikely to be disappointed. When Kerry gets his orders, he reports for duty.

The notion that such a man might preside over the democratic reform of American politics is a fantasy. He is frightened and flinching, a man who made the system work for him precisely by working for the system, and who knows perfectly well that it would destroy him the moment he turned against it. Compare him, for a moment, to Hugo Chávez — a leader with charisma, with a movement, with the visible enthusiasm of the poor behind him. Kerry has none of these things and wants none of them. A vote for Kerry, then, is not merely a vote against Bush. It is a vote for the survival of the system that made Bush possible.

Which brings me to Nader. I do not agree with all of his positions — on trade, for one, we part company. But no honest person can deny his courage. His decision to run despite the near-universal opposition of the progressive establishment is both brave and foolhardy, and I admire it for being both. He has spent his life fighting corporations and absorbing the abuse of a hostile media. He has been willing to take on the Anti-Defamation League, which he accuses of smearing critics of Israeli policy as antisemites — something no other American politician has dared to attempt.

He will not win. I know this. But that is to misunderstand the point. Systemic change must begin now, not in some perpetually deferred future when the circumstances are finally unexceptional. And the better Nader performs, the faster the movement for that change will grow. The Nation suggests he could do more good as a "public citizen," campaigning for open debates and rallying voters behind progressive Democrats. But what conceivable incentive would the Democrats have to listen? He holds leverage over these cowering creatures only for as long as they fear he will take their votes. Disarm him, and they will forget him within a week.

Let me be fair about the differences between the two men. Bush is active; Kerry is passive. Kerry wants high defence spending, but he would be more reluctant to attack other nations. He wants tax cuts, but he is less willing to gut health and education to pay for them. He wants lower oil prices, but he will not help the corporations prise open the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge. The differences are real, and they are not trivial.

The question is only this: are they enough to justify abandoning the sole electoral effort now under way to democratise American politics? They are not. What the progressive establishment is asking of you is that you trade liberty for security, democracy for expediency, the future for a slightly less frightening present. They mean well. They are wrong. Ignore them.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/validation/1/validation_5
body:
When Nick Raynsford, the planning minister, is accused of covering up his department's decision to allow a listed building in Oxford to be moved, so that Wafic Said, the arms broker, could build his business school upon the site, the minister has an unimpeachable alibi. He could not have concealed the decision, he can tell us, because he was not in the country when it was made. He was in Egypt, leading a trade mission. The charge, in other words, collapses before it is properly laid. But it seems to me that his alibi reveals a scandal far graver than the one of which he stands accused. For the real question is not whether a planning minister covered up a planning decision. The real question is what a planning minister was doing on a trade delegation to Egypt at all.

The delegation Raynsford led consisted of 27 corporations. Among them were Balfour Beatty, Enron, Laing, Mott MacDonald, Thames Water and WS Atkins. These companies had not travelled to Cairo to admire the pyramids. They had gone to seek contracts, and the contracts they sought were, in large part, connected to the Toshka project: a $90bn scheme to divert the Nile into the western desert. It is a project which has been condemned both by environmentalists, who point out that it will squander water Egypt cannot spare, and by financial consultants, who point out that it will squander money Egypt cannot spare. It is, in short, precisely the kind of grandiose, destructive, uneconomic development which, were it proposed in Britain, would fall within the regulatory purview of the very minister who had flown out to promote it.

There is a further oddity. The major contract at Toshka was awarded not to a British firm but to Kvaerner, which is Norwegian. This seems strange, until you discover that Kvaerner had, at the time, seconded more of its employees into the Department of the Environment, Transport and the Regions than any other company. The regulator, in other words, had been colonised by the regulated. It is not surprising that a department so staffed should work so diligently on behalf of the industry it is supposed to police.

This is not an aberration but a structure. Nick Raynsford is both the minister for planning, whose job is to regulate development, and the minister for construction, whose job is to promote it. He is, that is to say, simultaneously the poacher and the gamekeeper, and he resolves the conflict, as far as I can tell, invariably in the poacher's favour. He told Building magazine that his job was "creating a climate in which the industry can do well". He did not say that his job was creating a climate in which the public interest can do well.

The record bears him out. He sponsored a public relations campaign called "Minerals 98", whose purpose was to help the quarrying firms lobby against a proposed tax on roadstone. At Labour's local government conference in 1998 he argued against taxes on out-of-town parking, which the government's own transport policy required. He promised the British Council of Shopping Centres that he would speed up the approval of retail schemes. He watered down the energy efficiency regulations for new homes, on the grounds that they represented "an undue burden on builders". At every point at which the interests of the construction industry collided with the declared policy of the government he serves, Nick Raynsford appears to have chosen the industry.

But the gravest of all these allegations concerns a dam. Raynsford is said to have persuaded Tony Blair to back the Ilisu dam in Turkey: a project which will drown the Kurdish heartland and force 78,000 people from their homes, in what amounts to ethnic cleansing by inundation. In September 1999, Raynsford led a trade mission to Turkey, accompanying a consortium fronted by Balfour Beatty, which was seeking some £4bn in contracts to rebuild the areas devastated by that year's earthquake. Balfour Beatty also hopes to build the Ilisu dam. Three months later, the government announced that it was "minded to grant" export credit for the project. One is entitled to ask whether the earthquake contracts were made conditional upon Britain's support for the dam.

Which brings us back to the business school in Oxford. The real scandal, I think, is not that Raynsford concealed the decision to move the listed building. The real scandal is that everything he says about it is probably true. There was nothing unusual about the decision. There was nothing exceptional about the process. Turning a blind eye to the destruction of the environment and of our heritage, on behalf of the construction industry, is not something Nick Raynsford does when no one is looking. It is something he does every week, in plain sight, as a matter of policy. The man appointed to police the developers has become their most enthusiastic advocate. That is not an aberration to be exposed. It is a system to be dismantled.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_119
body:
I have been coming to America, off and on, for the best part of 30 years. I studied here, reported here, made friends and married one of them. I have written more words about this country than about my own. I thought I knew it. Then, this autumn, I discovered that I did not — and, more disconcertingly, that America was only now learning to know itself.

Perhaps a nation cannot know itself until something forces the question. Before September 11, the United States could afford the luxury of self-ignorance, the way rich men can afford to leave their attics unexamined. Now the attic has been ransacked, and everything has come tumbling out into daylight. I have spent Christmas in a remote, liberal corner of Vermont, and from this improbable vantage — snow to the windowsills, the nearest traffic light some miles distant — I have watched a country in the act of self-examination. My conclusion is simple, and I offer it with the audacity of a man generalising from one small place. America has not changed. It has become more so. Every virtue and every vice it possessed a year ago has intensified, clarified, and shed its ambiguity.

Take consideration. Americans were always considerate — the please and the thank-you, the have-a-nice-day that the visiting European mocks until he misses it. Now they are almost unbearably tender towards one another. The attack was collectively felt, and three months on the shock has not been stoically sloughed off, as an Englishman's would have been. People blunder about still, searching for meaning, and finding none, they simply care more. The response to incomprehension is not hardness but a redoubled gentleness.

Then there is self-confidence. No whining here, no self-pity, no picking at the wound. Self-sufficiency is second nature, and the American certainties that so maddened Osama bin Laden have not been dented but redoubled. Even the humour has come back, after a solemn interval. Garrison Keillor now makes jokes about jihad, which is to say that Islam has finally been dropped from the protective canopy of political correctness. That, in its way, is a form of national recovery.

Patriotism is the plainest of the intensifications. Flags always hung in Vermont yards; now they hang everywhere, and mean more. The irony is exquisite. This is a state that sends a self-described socialist to Congress and holds no great affection for George W. Bush. Yet the patriotic response to September 11 is unquestioning. There are few tourists here, and the papers are intensely local — the Valley News of White River Junction, which one might expect to lead with a church supper, now leads with Hamid Karzai's swearing-in in Kabul and carries news of Yasser Arafat's Christmas visit to Bethlehem. This is not, I think, a new internationalism. It is the shedding of an old indifference towards the world from which the threat comes. America's business is to protect America — and about that there is nothing to be ashamed.

On constitutionalism the change is more marked and, to me, more troubling. My liberal Vermont acquaintances, who would once have marched against far less, offer only mild objection to Bush's military tribunals and his curtailments of due process. The government cites Lincoln, who suspended habeas corpus, and Roosevelt, who curbed free speech, as precedents for what a threatened republic may do. This seems to satisfy people. Precedent has always been the American's comfort blanket, and he wraps himself in it now with a serenity that ought to give pause.

Meanwhile — and this is the curiosity — domestic politics carries on exactly as before. There are budget arguments, taxation debates, the left and the right jockeying for the 2002 elections, all the familiar apparatus of a functioning quarrel. But in foreign affairs, politics has virtually ceased. America has become easy to lead. Alarmingly so. Even those who distrust Bush trust him now, because there appears to be no alternative to trusting him. He vows to "get the job done" — that archetypal American phrase, at once reassuring and slightly frightening — and the country nods.

What strikes the visitor most, in the end, is the casual fatalism. Friends joke here about being "blown to pieces," the way one might joke about the weather. There is a sense that fate has taken the wheel, that America must simply do what it must do, which is to extinguish al-Qaida. American idealism, so fickle and so quickly bored in the diplomacy of the post-war decades, now has a purpose everyone agrees upon: the saving of America itself. What this means for the world is far from clear. But it is clearer than it has been since Jefferson what the world means for America — safety from sea to shining sea, and maybe not beyond.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_13
body:
Law and order is the one area of politics that is entirely about spin. Not partly, not mostly, but entirely. Everything the public thinks it knows, everything the politicians tell it, everything the newspapers scream, is a manufacture of impression rather than a distillation of fact. This is not to say that all the impressions are wrong. Some of them happen, by accident, to correspond with reality. Crime has roughly doubled over the past 20 years, which is true. The public does genuinely want heavier sentencing, which is also true. But the raw material with which the politicians deal, and out of which they fashion their competing appeals, is not fact. It is propaganda. In no other field of political conflict does impression so completely eclipse reality.

Consider the simplest test. The home secretary asserts that this government has presided over the best crime record in a generation. The shadow home secretary asserts, with equal solemnity, that the previous Conservative government did the same. Both claims are, within their own terms, defensible. It depends what years you choose to measure, what categories you include, and how you shade the figures. Take one set of dates and you prove one thing; take another and you prove the opposite. The voter, sitting at home, cannot possibly parse this. He is not equipped to. He is not meant to be. When Nick Davies and Roger Graef audited the government's record for Penguin, what emerged was not a triumph but a mixture: burglary down substantially, car theft down, violent crime down a little, but street robbery up, clear-up rates stuck where they always were, and convictions rising. This is the truth, and it is complicated. The trouble is that sophistication, however true, will always be outgunned by simplicity, however false.

The public, meanwhile, is startlingly uninformed. The British Crime Survey shows that most people believe judges are too soft, too lenient, too remote from ordinary life. Fair enough, one might think, until one discovers that the same public has almost no idea what sentences judges actually pass. Over half of those surveyed underestimate real sentences by at least 30%. They believe that around half of convicted rapists are sent to prison. The true figure is 97%. Similar grotesque misperceptions cluster around burglary and mugging. This ignorance is not innocent. It is the foundation on which the judiciary's reputation as a soft touch is built, an image brainlessly cultivated by a tabloid press that thrives on outrage, and which then seeps upward into ministerial critique and, from there, into policy itself. The spinners do not correct the misperception. They feed on it.

The legislative record tells the same story in a different key. The previous Conservative government passed a criminal justice act more or less every year, until the later statutes began quietly undoing the earlier ones. The present government's record is no more coherent. Curfew orders and anti-social behaviour orders, sensible enough on paper, have failed in practice, being either unused or unenforceable. The latest reform, allowing judges to take account of previous convictions when sentencing, is at least rational. It addresses a genuine finding: that a small population of perhaps 100,000 persistent offenders commits the bulk of recorded crime. It will spin well. It sounds tough and looks targeted. And the opposition will spin against it just as easily, warning of injustice to the reformed and the wrongly labelled. Both sides will perform. Neither will confront the problem.

There are three conclusions that no amount of spinning can address, and which the spinners therefore leave severely alone.

The first is that the best policies cost money. The current package is priced at around £500m. One is entitled to ask whether that is remotely sufficient to fund new custodial regimes, accelerated court hearings, and the increased police manpower that all of it presupposes. For the single most persuasive remedy across every one of the confusing statistics is more police officers on the streets. Yet the home secretary has conspicuously failed to meet his own recruitment promises. One wonders whether the Treasury is genuinely committed to the numbers, or whether the whole exercise is propaganda timed to a general election pencilled in for May 2005. The spending will be announced. Whether it materialises is another matter entirely.

The second is that there is a hole at the very centre of the crime agenda. Politicians are content to offer drugs as an explanation for crime. They will tell you, correctly, that a large proportion of acquisitive crime is committed to feed a habit. But they refuse, absolutely, to address the drug laws themselves as a focus for reform, despite mounting pressure from those best placed to know. To decriminalise cannabis would eliminate an entire category of crime at a single stroke, and free up police time and court time on a scale that no amount of curfew orders could match. It is the most obvious thing in the world. And it is precisely the thing the spinners dare not look at, because looking at it would require an argument rather than a slogan.

The third is that the prisons keep multiplying. Two statistics deserve to be placed side by side. Britain has the highest imprisonment rate in Europe. And England and Wales rank joint first among industrialised nations in crime as experienced by victims. No accountable politician will connect these two trends, because the connection is fatal to the entire rhetoric of toughness. If prison worked as advertised, the country that imprisons most should not also be the country most victimised. Recently the lord chief justice, the inspector of prisons, and the director of the prison service itself have all delivered clinical, sometimes passionate, critiques of a system straining under its own numbers. Their voices are respected and ignored in equal measure. The electoral auctioneers dare not change the terms of the debate, because the terms are the only thing they have.

For this is the nature of the spinner. He can appeal only to the lowest denominator of opinion, however ignorant, because that is the market he has helped to create and cannot now escape. He must feed the misperception he depends upon. And the spinner, more or less, always lies, not necessarily by stating what is false, but by arranging what is true until it produces an impression that is false. Both parties do it. Neither has any incentive to stop. The result is a politics in which the volume of noise about crime rises steadily while the quality of thought about it falls away to nothing.

The beginning of wisdom in the politics of law and order would be for someone, anyone, to start the long, thankless, arduous business of addressing the truth. It would win no headlines. It would please no editor. But it has become genuinely necessary, for a simple reason. We have reached the point at which nobody can any longer sort one spinner from another.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_16
body:
Anyone who has watched the Northern Ireland peace process crawl through its long agony finds it hard to believe it might fail now.
