After so much distance travelled, so many barriers surmounted, so many funerals attended and speeches endured, disbelief is the natural response to the thought that the whole thing might collapse at the last. Yet the end game is always the hardest part. That is the paradox we must now confront. The conflict, at this stage, is no longer about power, or religion, or even the possession of territory. It is about attitudes towards mutual surrender: who will step back, who will be seen to yield, who will endure the humiliation the other side seems to require. What is now ranged against itself is not falsehood against truth. It is two forms of real sincerity, each impregnable in its own conviction. That is precisely why the challenge may defeat them both.

Take the republican side first. There is, among these people, a genuine belief that the war is over. Sinn Fein-IRA, under Gerry Adams and Martin McGuinness, has made a historic shift onto the political track, and it would be foolish to pretend the shift is fraudulent. The IRA has asserted its "total" commitment to a "permanent peace", words that would once have been unthinkable in the republican lexicon. The Adams argument runs like this. Republicanism has abandoned its constitutional claim to a united Ireland by force. It sits in a government it once sought to destroy. It talks, civilly and continually, with the British state it spent thirty years trying to bomb into concession. What more, its leaders ask, is anyone entitled to demand? The very slowness with which they came to this position, the decades in which they resisted it with such murderous obstinacy, is what makes the U-turn credible. Men do not abandon a lifetime's faith on a whim. And on decommissioning, the republican view is coolly logical. It is achievable, they say, but beside the point. The war has de facto ended. That, not the handing over of a few rifles, is what matters in the real world.

Now consider the unionists, who have shown a sincerity fully equal to the other side's. They have accepted former terrorists into the government of the province, and begun the extraordinary business of running the country alongside them. To do this, they have overcome obstacles buried deep in the unionist psyche, obstacles of history, of grief, of tribal memory that outsiders can scarcely imagine. They have done it under constant pressure from hate-filled supporters, and from parts of the London press that would rather like the enterprise to fail, the better to prove their prophecies of doom. David Trimble deserves to be called a statesman for the way he has manoeuvred his people past their own most obdurate negatives. He and his party have already lived with prolonged procrastination over arms; they will, in the end, swallow most of the Patten report on the reform of the RUC, a document that touches the unionist heart as nearly as anything could. These are not the concessions of cynics. They are the concessions of men who have decided, at real cost, to make peace work.

There is a temptation, felt keenly by outsiders not steeped in the terrible particularities of Ulster, to say the answer is obvious. Let the unionists take one more stride. Let them enter government, or remain in it, even without decommissioning delivered by the May date. Having swallowed the camel of former killers in the executive, are they not now straining ludicrously at the gnat of a symbolic handover? And there is the darker argument, pressed with some force: that to insist upon Trimble's resignation if General de Chastelaine cannot certify that arms have begun to be given up is to put blood on unionist hands, to sacrifice the whole edifice of the Good Friday agreement for the sake of a gesture that changes nothing on the ground.

I do not accept that it is insensitive, at this hour, to speak of blame. In the hour of crisis it is the republicans who are on trial, and it does no service to peace to pretend otherwise. Realpolitik has carried us a long way. It has taught the unionists to live with the intolerable and the republicans to renounce the sacred. But Realpolitik has now reached its limit. What is required from Sinn Fein-IRA is a decisive — though entirely incomplete — surrender: a token handing over of weapons that would leave the arsenals overwhelmingly intact. Substantively, such a gesture is meaningless. Nobody supposes it disarms the movement. Everybody understands the dumps would remain, mapped and waiting. But politically it is everything, because it is proof. In the real world a promise is not enough. Words of permanent peace, however sincerely meant, do not bind a movement that has broken its word before and could break it again. Only the act binds. Only the unbearable discomfort of a necessary gesture demonstrates that republicanism has genuinely entered the world it says it has chosen to belong to — the world of politics, of accountability, of consequences borne rather than evaded.

That is the crux. The unionists have already paid in the coin of their own history. They have accepted a settlement that offends every instinct their community holds. What they ask in return is not the impossible. It is not total decommissioning, which everyone privately concedes will never come. It is a symbol, and a symbol is what politics runs upon. To offer it would cost the IRA nothing but its pride, and the surrender of pride is the very substance of every peace ever made between enemies who have hated one another. To withhold it, while demanding that Trimble go on making concession after concession, is to insist that only one side need feel discomfort — that republican dignity is sacrosanct while unionist dignity is expendable.

Everything else is bunker piety. It is the language of men who prefer the purity of the fortress to the messiness of the compromise they have already, in principle, agreed to make. The peace process has come too far to founder on it. But founder it may, unless the movement that chose the path of politics is willing to take the one small step that proves it meant what it said.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/validation/2/validation_11
body:
All parties now agree that ideology is dead. The word is as dirty to the Tories fighting out their leadership contest as it is to Tony Blair delivering a major address on the future of public services. And who is not pleased to hear it? But there is a downside. Having no ideology means that all aspects of politics become more perplexing. Government is complicated to do and just as complicated to understand. The silver thread that once linked and determined everything has gone. When the defining mantra of the left said public good and private bad, and the right said roughly the opposite, politics was easy to make sense of. Now everything is messier.

While the practitioners of the trade have eventually had no alternative but to recognise this, public understanding has not caught up. It strives to cling to the old simplicities. The mindset of ideology survives the passing of the ideological age. It is not merely in newspaper headlines, groping for the familiar matrix, that Blairism's plans for public-service delivery are seen as a private-sector takeover. I would bet most people in the country see it that way too.

For this the government is largely to blame. In its first term, feeling its way forward, it was not straight. It wanted to please all the people all the time, so it tended to reassure the business world with one spin about innovation and competition, while distracting its own constituency with words that said nothing fundamental would change. Its attitude to the health service covered a gamut of promises from tough to tender. Its replacement weapon for ideology was "what works". But this became its philosophy well before it had the faintest idea what worked in either health or education. It knew that what the Tories had done, especially in the NHS, did not work, but was far from sure how to replace it.

There remains a lot of confusion. Important differences are still not widely apprehended, partly because ministers' own schemes reflect the complication of post-ideological government, and partly because producer unions want to make things seem more threatening than they are. Enlisting private-sector skills and money to develop surgical centres or improve failing schools is not the same as privatisation.

Very little of the public sector is capable of being run by the private sector in any case. Instituting regimes of competition or "contestability", a practice common in the public sectors of France, Germany, Holland and other continental models, is not an assault on public-service principles but a minimal necessity for raising them to the best-practice levels everyone is entitled to expect.

Yesterday, Mr Blair got closer to clarifying some of this than he has done before. It was the most important speech he has made since the election. Though the news line will be his commitment not to "flinch" from producer union opposition, the real message was a more cogent invitation than ever before to move out of the ideological box. Some of the unions believe he is thirsting for a fight, even looking for a demonstration combat such as Mrs Thatcher had with the miners. Actually his words sounded as though they came from the last-chance saloon of the British version of social democracy, which has rather suddenly woken up to the danger of its core proposition being seen to fail.

It was a sobering not a militant address. The case it made chimes with the experience of most people who use public services of every kind, which tells them that while more money is absolutely necessary, it is not necessarily sufficient, and that reform is essential if delivery is going to improve. This was an inaugural moment for the second term in another way as well. It put into operation the vow of plodding, responsible realism with which Mr Blair said he would replace the ever-deceptive spinning that was the leitmotif of 1997-2001. As such it sent three signals.

First, the government has no single plan for public services. There is no Great Blueprint, the temptation towards which it was lured in the first term. Compared with some of its first-term rhetoric, the glorification of the private has actually receded. There are different answers for different problems. If more than half of local education authorities are described by Ofsted as either fairly or very successful, as they are, the sweeping abolition favoured by some reformers makes no sense. It makes as little sense to insist that only NHS units may tender to run the new surgical centres just because they are being set up as a supplement to NHS hospitals.

A senior trade unionist told me yesterday that he was sceptical of ministers' ability to get down to the detail of reform. They were, he said, more at home talking about, and endlessly revising, their fancy institutional structures than with the tedious practicalities. He also regretted the adversarial - anti-union - subtext that seemed to have made its way into the discourse. But Blair avoided that. His real subtext was that, at a time of peace and high employment, no task of government is in greater public demand than making essential public services, the base material of a functioning society, work properly.

Second, it is without doubt true that only a social democratic government has a chance of doing this. Only the party with an incontestable belief in the primacy of public services can be trusted to reform their delivery in ways that do not merely profit private business and ultimately deprive the less advantaged members of society. As a matter of fact, this belief may be a little more widespread than it was: another victory, perhaps, for a new spirit of the times. Two of the candidates for the Tory party leadership make almost everything of their commitment to health and education, and admit that it was failure in this field that contributed most to the public's hatred of their party at the election.

But the third candidate might yet win. Iain Duncan Smith is the voice of the hard Tory right, which flirts with the minimal state, and much of which favours an American-style approach. With that line, IDS would be even less likely than Michael Portillo or Kenneth Clarke to win the next election. But the state of Conservatism makes plain the danger that, without visible improvements brought about by progressive politicians, the public sector will become the source of yet deeper cynicism and despair.

Thirdly, though, the challenge is in one sense impossible to meet. Blair said yesterday that it was "as great as any that has faced a postwar government". We have to be aware that he has staked everything on outcomes that will, in many people's eyes, never be satisfactory. The famous glass, whether in school or hospital, is always in danger of being half-empty. If the world economy, dragging Britain's with it, faces a prolonged downturn, not even the prudence of Mr Brown will have been enough to preserve the required budget surplus.

All one can say at the moment is that Mr Blair has set out, without glamour or spin, on the defining stage of his engagement with the realities the people most value. Even that is worth saying, however - and worth supporting.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/validation/2/validation_12
body:
Between one mandate and the next, before the machinery of a fresh election blots out memory of what the last one was for, is the moment to conduct an audit. Not of policies won or lost, nor of the tally of promises kept, but of something more elusive: what power has done to the moral perception of the people who wield it. The question is not whether ministers have grown harder in the arteries. That is inevitable, almost forgivable. The question is whether the gates of their own self-awareness have quietly swung shut, so that they can no longer see themselves. And on the evidence of what this government has done to immigration law, the answer is that they cannot.

Consider what these people believed when they arrived. They came to office professing an article of faith about racial justice more fervent than any government before them. One of the first things they did was to abolish a rule, inherited from their predecessors, which had targeted the spouses of migrants for special suspicion. They convened a landmark inquiry into a racially motivated murder, a proceeding that was meant to hold up a mirror to the whole apparatus of the state and ask whether racism had seeped into its institutions. The leading figures treated this cause as sacred. To question their commitment to it was, in those days, almost blasphemous. It was, in a word, unimaginable that they would ever do the thing they had spent their political lives condemning.

And yet. Buried in the race relations legislation they were so proud of was a clause that exempted the immigration service from the anti-discrimination rules that bound everyone else. The state's own gatekeepers were placed beyond the reach of the very principle the statute existed to enforce. Then came a ministerial order, authorising officials to treat certain nationalities differently from others — which nationalities left conveniently unspecified. When the point was raised, the responsible minister waved it away. A technicality, he called it. The attack, he said, was bizarre. Here was the first tell. A man who once made racial equality his creed could look at an exemption for discrimination and see nothing but a piece of administrative housekeeping.

Worse followed. A second order arrived, and this one did not bother with the decent vagueness of "unspecified". It named the groups. Kurds, Roma, Albanians, Tamils, Pontic Greeks, Somalis, Afghans: these were the classes of person to be singled out for what the language called "more rigorous examination". Not as individuals who had aroused suspicion. As classes. As types. To belong to one of these peoples was, by official instruction, to be presumed more likely an illegal immigrant or a bogus asylum-seeker, and to be treated accordingly at the frontier.

Pause over the vicious absurdity of it. Several of the named groups possess no passport of their own, because they possess no nation-state. There is no Kurdish passport. There is no Roma one. How, then, is an official to identify a member of these classes for his more rigorous attention? Only by appearance. Only by inference from a face, a name, a manner of speech. What the order does, in plain terms, is write the logic of racial profiling into statute, and dress it in the sober vocabulary of examination. It gets worse still. A sub-clause provides that if the information an applicant needs is not available in a language the applicant understands, the official is under no duty to supply it in one that he does. Cruelty is thus not merely permitted but formalised, tidied into a paragraph.

There have been two phases to the deception. During the passage of the bill, ministers assured us the power was benign — that it existed only to discriminate in favour of groups, to fast-track Kosovan refugees and the like, to help not to harm. This was a lie, and it should be named as one. For once the thing was on the statute book, the minister responsible admitted, almost cheerfully, that the order merely formalised what the immigration service had been doing for years. His tone was one of flippant bewilderment that anyone should object. Long-standing practice, he seemed to say. Nothing new here. What had been sold as protection was revealed as institutionalised suspicion, and the salesman could not fathom the complaint.

Three conclusions stare out. The first is that ethnic selection is now systematic and official. There is, it is true, a "race monitor" appointed to keep watch. But the monitor examines matters only retrospectively, and looks at no individual case. He is a figurehead, a piece of public relations placed at the door for reassurance, powerless by design. The second conclusion is that this is cowardice. The government is caving to a hardline opposition figure whose rhetoric on asylum has poisoned the well, and rather than face him down it collaborates in his discourse — the discourse that treats the very act of seeking asylum as presumptively fraudulent, and the asylum-seeker as a cheat until proven otherwise. The third, and the darkest, is that something has rotted in the ruling party itself. They have become as bad as the predecessors they despised, while losing the intellectual equipment to recognise the fact. The party barely protested. The lead minister, a self-styled anti-racist to the marrow, genuinely cannot see the contradiction. Ask him and he will look at you with the honest puzzlement of a man who knows his own heart is pure.

That is the corruption, and it is more insidious than any conspiracy. There is no cabal here plotting to do harm behind closed doors. There is instead a wall of righteous certainty — the settled conviction that, because of who they are and what they believe, these people cannot possibly be doing wrong. Their anti-racism is not a discipline to be tested against their conduct; it is a fixed property of their souls, which their conduct cannot touch. And so the perception closes over. The gate swings shut. They do the thing they condemned, and see instead the thing they intended.

This law is a disgrace. Let that be said without qualification. And here is the final, bitter irony: perhaps only a party so utterly convinced of its own anti-racism would have found the nerve to smuggle such a scheme of ethnic punishment across the line that separates decency from shame. A guiltier conscience would have flinched. Theirs did not, because it did not know it had anything to flinch from.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_127
body:
There are certain melodies that can wake you in the small hours. Flick to the right channel late enough at night and you might catch them still: those old black-and-white broadcasts, the kibbutz choirs in full song. The men wear open work shirts, the women peasant skirts and headscarves, and they stand in serried, earnest rows before an audience that beams up at them, the whole tableau bathed in the honest glow of Zionist certainty. They sing the great Hebrew folk songs, the melodies that once seemed to hum in every corner of the young state: songs of the land, of the harvest, of the dew on the fields at dawn. It is unbearably corny now, this socialist-realist kitsch, all pioneering vigour and communal joy.

And yet, as Israel this week marks its 60th birthday, not with kibbutz choirs but with beach barbecues, DJ sets and street parties from Eilat to Haifa, those old reruns offer a more revealing window into what this country was — and what it has become — than any of the official festivities. For the argument I want to make is a double one, and it will annoy both sides. Israel is chronically misunderstood by its modern critics; it was never the imperial adventure they imagine. But today's Israelis have wandered so far from their founding ideals, retreating from the collective into a private, comfortable "bubble", that they have lost the one thing peace requires: urgency.

Start with the critics. The fashionable case runs that Israel was simply an act of western imperialism, a European enclave dropped into the Middle East, no different in kind from the white settlers of Australia, Rhodesia or South Africa. It is a seductive charge, but it collapses the moment you look at the faces. Walk down any Israeli street and you will find Moroccans and Iraqis, Russians and Romanians, Ethiopians who trekked out of Africa, Kurds and Yemenites and Persians. To call this a European colony is to know nothing of it. The pied-noir of Algeria had a France to go back to. These people had nowhere.

That is the meaning of the great anthem "Ein Li Eretz Acheret" — "I have no other land". It is not a boast of conquest but a cry of desperation, and here lies the tragedy the song's admirers rarely acknowledge: the Palestinian feels exactly the same. He, too, has no other land. Two peoples, one soil, each with nowhere else to go — that is the whole conflict in a single line of Hebrew.

Remember, too, the timing. The Jewish state was declared just three years after the liberation of Auschwitz, at the one moment when the world, appalled by what it had allowed to happen, briefly conceded that the Jews had the same right as anyone else: a roof, a home, a place. Recall Yossi Harel, the young captain of the Exodus, which in 1947 carried 4,500 survivors of Hitler's camps toward the shore of Palestine — only to be turned back by the British and, in an act of grotesque cruelty, sent all the way back to Germany. That is not the story of a landowner seeking a second estate or a nation hungry for resources. It is the story of a homeless man, half-dead, banging on a door in the rain. The pioneers of America and Africa came looking for more. The Jews of the 1940s came looking for anything at all.

So the choirs sang. And here the old reruns tell their second truth, for early Israel was less like Rhodesia than like East Germany. Socialism was not a policy; it was the state religion. The kibbutz was its cathedral. Everything was collective, and often absurdly so. Floor tiles came in a handful of identical patterns, mass-produced, so that half the country lived on the same floor. A single kibbutz, by common arrangement, manufactured every toilet seat in the land. Foreign investors were required to hand over a 51% stake in their companies to the Histadrut, the great trade union congress that functioned as employer, insurer and state within a state. This was a country that believed, with a fervour those beaming choirs make plain, in the "we" over the "I".

That "we" has all but vanished. The kibbutzim themselves led the retreat. Today most have privatised: members own their own homes, earn different wages according to their work, hire outside labour, run their guesthouses as businesses. The commune has become a co-operative, and often merely a suburb with a swimming pool. And the kibbutz is the country in miniature. Israel as a whole has privatised, its citizens withdrawing steadily from the collective sphere into the fortress of private life.

They have a word for that fortress: the bu'ah, the bubble. It is the psychic membrane the Israeli constructs to keep the anxiety out — the anxiety of a security situation that never lets up. Inside the bubble there is the café, the mortgage, the hi-tech start-up, the beach. Outside, there is the war that could come tomorrow. The polling captures the schizophrenia perfectly. Nearly 40% of Israelis believe their country faces a genuine, existential threat of destruction. And yet 83% report themselves satisfied, even happy, with their own personal lives. It is possible, apparently, to fear annihilation and enjoy your weekend, provided the two thoughts are kept in separate rooms.

This is the state of mind Israel brings to its 60th year — and to the peace process. The US administration has set its deadline: progress at the Annapolis talks in time for George Bush's farewell visit to Jerusalem. And Israel insists, loudly, that it is straining for a settlement. Foreign Minister Tzipi Livni takes visible pride in the fact that she keeps negotiating with the Palestinians even on "days of terror". Prime Minister Ehud Olmert's hawkish critics complain that his peacemaking is a smokescreen, a distraction from the corruption inquiry closing around him.

But strip away the rhetoric and the urgency is not there. When Jimmy Carter arrived in Jerusalem bearing messages from Hamas and from Damascus, not a single frontline Israeli minister would agree to meet him. Israel says it cannot legitimise Hamas for fear of undermining President Mahmoud Abbas — and yet Olmert has done next to nothing to strengthen Abbas. He has not dismantled the illegal West Bank outposts. He has not offered to compensate settlers willing to leave. The gestures that might make Abbas credible to his own people remain, year after year, ungiven.

The mood is cynical, and the cynicism has an argument. We withdrew from Lebanon in 2000, Israelis say, and got Hezbollah's Katyushas. We withdrew from Gaza in 2005 and got Hamas's Qassams. Why should we believe that giving up more land buys us anything but rockets closer to home? And, crucially, they have concluded they can live without peace. The separation barrier has choked off the suicide bombings; the buses are safe again. The demographic threat — the day when Arabs outnumber Jews between the river and the sea — is real, but it is abstract, a graph rather than a wound, and abstractions do not move governments. A strong economy, growing at 5.1%, cushions everything. Why risk the bubble?

So the anniversary comes, prosperous and complacent, and the choirs sing on, at least for the few insomniacs still awake to hear them. And of all the melodies drifting out of those old broadcasts, one now sounds more passé than any other, more out of step with the mood of the country that once sang it with such conviction: "Shir L'shalom" — the song for peace.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_22
body:
Apologies, I have always thought, are far easier to give than to receive. The one who does the apologising claims the moral high ground: he has confronted his sins, made his confession and emerged, cleansed, on the other side. He feels good about himself. It is the recipient who is left in the awkward position. For he has to decide, there and then, whether to forgive – and if his heart is not yet ready, if the wound is still raw, he risks looking like the churlish one, the obstinate holdout who cannot find it within himself to move on. The apologiser walks away purified; the wronged party is left holding a grudge that now looks like petulance.

This is true across the kitchen table, in the messy business of family quarrels and cooling marriages. But it is true, too, on the far larger stage of Northern Ireland. For this week the IRA issued an apology – and it is the unionists, the recipients of that apology, who find themselves trapped in exactly that unhappy position.

The statement itself was a curious document. Its language was borrowed less from the pulpit or the barracks than from the therapist's couch: "hurt", "pain", "grief". The IRA offered its "sincere apologies and condolences" to the families of "non-combatants" killed and injured over the course of the Troubles. Notably, it declined to apologise for anything else, and it pointedly refused to include the British government among those to whom regret was due. The timing was hardly accidental: the statement arrived as the 30th anniversary of Bloody Friday approached – that afternoon in July 1972 when the IRA detonated some 20 bombs across Belfast, killing nine people and mangling many more.

The first reactions split the unionist community neatly down the middle. Tom Donnelly, whose sister was killed on Bloody Friday, said he was moved, that he detected something genuine in the words, that perhaps a corner had been turned. But he was in the minority. Most unionist voices dismissed the whole exercise as, in the phrase that recurred again and again, "pure PR" – a stunt, a manoeuvre, calibrated for maximum political effect and minimal moral cost.

And here the reason for the cynicism becomes clear. For next Wednesday the British government must deliver its verdict on whether the IRA is honouring its ceasefire. If London decides it is not, then David Trimble will demand the expulsion of Sinn Féin from the Northern Ireland executive – and the whole edifice of the Good Friday agreement could come tumbling down. Seen against that deadline, the apology looks less like an act of contrition than a well-timed insurance policy: a gesture designed to soften the ground before the verdict comes in.

The unionist loss of faith did not come from nowhere. For many of them, the 1998 agreement was always a moral outrage – the sight of former paramilitaries walking free, sharing power, sitting in government. They swallowed it only in the hope that republicans would, in return, put violence behind them for good. That hope has been steadily dismantled. First came the arrest in Colombia of three republicans in the company of the Farc guerrillas. Then the break-in at the Castlereagh intelligence base. Then the continuing, grinding violence on the streets of Belfast. Each episode chipped away at unionist belief until little was left. Were assembly elections held tomorrow, the anti-agreement DUP of Ian Paisley would sweep aside Trimble's UUP.

What the unionists want now is a US envoy, appointed by George Bush, to come and assess whether the ceasefire is genuinely being observed. Such a figure, they calculate, would reassure their sceptical base and buy Trimble precious time – the political oxygen he needs simply to survive.

The republicans, for their part, will have none of it. They approach the Good Friday agreement with something close to jesuitical rigour: if a thing is not written explicitly into the text, it does not exist. A US envoy? Not in the agreement. A "widening" of the ceasefire's terms? Not in the agreement. They expect Tony Blair to deliver a verdict artful enough to keep Sinn Féin in government – ambiguous where it needs to be, firm where it can afford to be. And there is a distinctly choreographed quality to the whole affair, the timing between the IRA, London and Dublin fitting together a little too neatly for coincidence.

Still, in fairness to the republicans, the apology was a big deal. It is no small thing for a movement that styles itself a liberation army, that believes to its bones that its cause was just, to express regret for the blood it has spilled. Cast your mind back only a few years: Sinn Féin could barely bring itself to mumble a word of contrition for anything. Now the IRA itself apologises. That is movement, and it should not be waved away.

Consider a thought experiment. Imagine it is September 2031 – thirty years on from the attacks of 11 September 2001 – and the ruling council of al-Qaida issues a statement expressing regret for what it did to the "non-combatants" of New York and Washington. Would that heal the families of the dead? Almost certainly not. But now add one further detail: suppose al-Qaida had been on ceasefire for six or eight years by then. Wouldn't the world breathe a little easier at such a statement? Wouldn't it be read, correctly, as a signal about intent – proof that something in the organisation's calculus had shifted?

That is not to pretend the apology is all it might have been. It should, by rights, have extended to "combatants" too – to the RUC officers and soldiers cut down over three decades, who were somebody's sons and husbands and fathers. To exclude them is to keep alive the fiction that some deaths were legitimate and others regrettable. That is a real flaw.

But ask, finally, what sinister purpose these supposed "ploys" are actually serving. The answer is almost embarrassingly modest: to keep republicans in government, exactly as the agreement permits. Decommissioning and the apology alike serve one end and one end only – keeping the peace process afloat, now that republicanism has concluded, coldly and strategically, that peace serves its interests better than war. That is not a cause for cynicism. It is the whole point.

What we are watching is republicanism on a long, slow, incomplete journey from its paramilitary past towards a purely political future. It is not neat, and it is certainly not perfect. But it is more real with each passing month. And the unionists, offered this imperfect apology, face the recipient's ancient dilemma. Sometimes, in peace processes as in life, the hardest and the wisest thing is to know how to say yes.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_25
body:
"If it ain't broke, don't fix it." For as long as anyone can remember, this has been the entire case for Britain's unwritten constitution, delivered with a shrug and a knowing smile. The argument is always the same. Look at the ancient patchwork of custom, convention and tradition — the bits of parchment stretching back to Magna Carta, the unspoken understandings that somehow keep the whole show on the road — and marvel that it has survived. No revolutions, no tanks on the lawn, no dictators. It has lasted centuries, and centuries of survival must count for something. To go poking around inside such a delicate mechanism would be reckless vandalism. Leave well alone.

It is a seductive argument, and it has held the field for a very long time. But it rests on a single, unexamined premise: that the thing works. And here is the awkward truth that its defenders would rather not confront. It doesn't. The British constitution is broken — not metaphorically, not in some abstract seminar-room sense, but demonstrably, visibly, in the daily quality of the governance it produces. And that, in the end, is the only test that matters. A constitution is not a museum piece to be admired for its age. It is a machine for governing a country well. Judge ours by that standard and it fails.

**Broken in the doing**

Consider the record. In the space of a decade and a half we have lived through a catalogue of calamities that would shame any competent state. The pensions scandal, in which hundreds of thousands were mis-sold policies and swindled of their savings. The BSE crisis, in which ministers assured us British beef was perfectly safe even as the science screamed otherwise. Parliamentary sleaze, cash slipped into brown envelopes, questions asked for money. The great railway privatisation, a botch so comprehensive it managed to combine higher fares, worse service and public subsidy all at once. The poll tax, which brought people onto the streets and a prime minister to her knees. And, lest we forget, the Millennium Dome — that great, empty tent, a monument to a governing class that could spend a fortune and produce nothing.

The tempting response is to treat each of these as a one-off, a piece of bad luck or bad judgment, the fault of this minister or that administration. But that is precisely the mistake. String them together and a pattern emerges: these were not isolated blunders but systemic failures. In each case the machinery of scrutiny, of checks and balances, of accountability, simply was not there. Decisions were taken in secret, by a tiny executive answerable to almost no one, with a Parliament too supine and a second chamber too absurd to hold anyone to account. The Scott inquiry into arms-for-Iraq and the Phillips report into BSE told us the same thing, in the sober language of the official document: the flaw lies not in the individuals but in the system itself. That is as authoritative a diagnosis as we are ever likely to get. The constitution is broken.

**The people have noticed**

And the public, whatever the constitutional priesthood may believe, has worked this out for itself. Confidence in the House of Commons roughly halved between 1985 and 1995. A 1996 poll across the European Union placed Britons' faith in their own parliament near the very bottom of the member states — a remarkable result for a nation that never tires of lecturing the continent on the mother of parliaments. Local election turnout has been sinking towards the derisory; a recent by-election in Leeds drew fewer than one in five voters to the polls. A Mori survey found 71% of first-time voters believed their ballot would make little or no difference to anything.

That is not apathy. That is a verdict. Citizens are voting with their feet, and what they are telling us is that they no longer believe the system represents them or responds to them. A constitution that has forfeited the confidence of the governed is not, whatever its antiquity, a functioning one.

**Labour has already changed everything**

Here is where the "leave well alone" brigade run into a difficulty of their own making. Since 1997, Labour has undertaken constitutional surgery of an order for which historians must reach back to the Great Reform Act of the 1830s. Devolution to Scotland, to Wales, to Northern Ireland. The removal of most hereditary peers from the House of Lords. Whatever else one says of it, this is not tinkering. It is the most thoroughgoing rearrangement of the British state in a century and a half.

And it has shattered the old myth for good. The conservative case always depended on the notion of an untouchable, mystical constitution, too subtle and organic to be interfered with by mere legislators. But Labour has interfered with it, comprehensively — which means it can be interfered with, and therefore that the only remaining question is whether we do the job properly or leave it half-done. The patchwork quilt is already unravelling in our hands. You cannot pull out that many threads and then piously insist the fabric must not be touched.

**Four nations**

Take devolution first. Britain has always governed itself as a unitary state, run from Westminster, as though it were a single nation. Devolution has exposed what was in fact always true: that Britain is a multi-national entity, a nascent federation. But having opened that question, Labour has conspicuously declined to answer it. What, exactly, is to become of England? Is Westminster to be a federal legislature for the whole United Kingdom, or is it also to double as an English parliament — and if both, how? Why should Scotland enjoy powers denied to the other nations? And there sits the West Lothian question, unanswered and unanswerable within the present muddle: why should a Scottish MP be entitled to vote on matters affecting only England, when an English MP may not vote on the equivalent matters devolved to Edinburgh? The worms were always in the can. Labour has now prised the lid off, and stood back.

**Shattered mystique**

The Lords make the point still more sharply. Sweeping away most of the hereditaries was meant to cure the second chamber. Instead it has exposed the disease. When the place was stuffed with dukes and earls who happened to have inherited a seat in the legislature of a modern democracy, one could dismiss the whole thing as a Ruritanian joke, a harmless survival to be smiled at. Now we are confronted with something worse: a supposedly reformed chamber in which not a single member has been elected by anybody. The old Tory position — of leaving the ancient absurdity undisturbed — is dead. Once you have begun, you must finish, and finish democratically. Even William Hague's Conservatives, hardly a hotbed of radicalism, are now flirting with the idea of an English parliament. The present hybrid — old custom crossed with New Labour half-measures — risks being the worst of both worlds: neither venerable nor legitimate.

**The wider storm**

And all this is happening as the ground shifts beneath the whole idea of who governs whom. At Nice the European Union is debating its own future shape — majority voting against national vetoes, a single currency, a shared military capacity, even a basic law for Europe. Widen the lens further and the streets of Seattle and Prague are filled with protesters demanding to know who really rules the world: the people, or the corporations and the World Trade Organisation. In every arena, from the local council chamber to the global summit, the same question keeps surfacing: who should be in charge? That is the question of the age — for the world, for Europe and, most urgently, for Britain.

**The moment of decision**

Three pressures, then, converging at once. An old system that has failed the basic test of good governance. A set of Labour reforms that have opened profound questions while resolving none of them. And a world convulsed by the deepest question of all — the question of sovereignty. Each on its own might be managed. Together they amount to an unanswerable case.

A new, written constitutional settlement would meet all three. It would furnish the checks and scrutiny whose absence gave us the poll tax and BSE. It would complete the business Labour has left dangerously half-finished, settling the relations between the four nations before they tear themselves apart. And it would allow Britain, at last, to speak clearly about its place in Europe and the world. For here is the deepest point of all: we cannot possibly clarify our relationship to Brussels, or to the wider global order, while we remain so utterly confused about the location of sovereignty within our own borders. A country that does not know who rules it is in no position to negotiate about who should rule beyond it.

The old defence has run its course. The constitution is not unbroken; it is breaking before our eyes, and the union itself may not survive our refusal to face the fact. The moth-eaten garb of the past no longer fits the country we have become. A new century, a new millennium, demand something better: a settlement written down, agreed, and owned by the people it governs. It is time to stop admiring the antique and start building the modern. It is time for a written constitution.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/train/3/train_27
body:
Every year, and with good reason, the great institutions of British life submit themselves to appraisal. Schools have their inspections, hospitals their ratings, corporations their annual reports, all designed to establish whether the promises made twelve months earlier bore any resemblance to what actually came to pass. A columnist, who spends his days handing out grades to prime ministers and presidents, generals and mayors, ought not to be exempt from the same scrutiny. And so, in a spirit of masochism dressed up as accountability, allow me to conduct a performance review of my own.

Exactly one year ago, this column offered a set of reckless predictions for the year ahead. Now comes the reckoning - the moment to establish where I hit the target and where I missed so badly that a decent employer might quietly suggest I take up a different line of work.

**1. The judicial inquiry.** I predicted that the leader would survive the judge's findings, and that he did. I predicted, too, that a media organisation would be faulted for sloppy procedures, and that also came to pass. So far, so smug. But then I made my error. I assumed the judge would spread the pain evenly, dishing out a portion of blame to each side, government and broadcaster alike, in the manner of a nervous referee anxious to send everybody home unhappy in equal measure. He did no such thing. He inflicted damage on one party alone, and on the central controversy - the very ground I had confidently predicted he would tiptoe around - he ruled with a boldness that left the guilty side reeling and the other whistling as it strolled away. Grade: partial credit, marked down for cowardice of imagination.

**2. The American election.** I called the winner correctly, which sounds impressive until you learn that I badly misidentified the man he beat. I had backed a rival to emerge from the primaries, a candidate the insiders assured me had the momentum, the money and the message. He never made it to the general at all. My winner won; my loser lost a contest he was never in. Half marks, and a red face.

**3. The military occupation.** Here I was right where it was grim and wrong where it was hopeful. I predicted the violence would continue and dismissed the giddy optimism that followed the dictator's capture, and both judgments held. But I wrongly expected the occupying power, mindful of its own electoral calendar, to timetable elections and begin bringing the troops home for domestic political comfort. It did neither. The cynicism was correct; the cynicism was simply not deep enough.

**4. Israel and Palestine.** I sensed, rightly, that the incumbent's old approach would come to be seen as futile, a consensus hardening from the military elites down. What I did not foresee was that the leader himself would be the one to seize the new strategy, rather than being dragged from office by it. I had confidently predicted that the corruption allegations swirling around him would engulf him and force his resignation. They swirled. He stayed.

**5. The domestic opposition.** I predicted a solid year for the opposition leader, and here I can point, with some straining, to a technical success: his party did top a nationwide poll. But the vote share behind that victory was so historically dismal that the word "triumph" curdled in the mouth. It was less a mandate than a survival note. Barely a pass.

**6. And the domestic mavericks.** These I got right, which is why I mention them last, greedily. I predicted the maverick would be readmitted to his party and re-elected as a city mayor, and so he was. And I predicted that the powerful cabinet figure would remain in his post, doing what he does best - waiting. He is waiting still.

So what, stepping back, has this exercise taught me? Two lessons, and neither is comfortable.

The first is that I should have distrusted the conventional wisdom of the political insiders. My humiliation over the primary is the proof. I backed the candidate the clever people were backing, in the salons and the green rooms, and the clever people were wrong. The lesson, painfully familiar and painfully unlearned, is that those closest to a process are often the last to see it clearly.

The second lesson is stranger, and rather shaming for a journalist who prides himself on a certain hard-bitten scepticism. I was not cynical enough. Look again at my errors. I expected the inquiry to apportion blame fairly. I expected the occupying power to reward its own citizens with a withdrawal. I expected corruption to end a career. In each case I assumed that the mechanisms of accountability - the judge, the ballot box, the prosecutor - would function as they are advertised to function: fairly, predictably, consequentially. And in each case they did not.

That, more than any single event, was the theme of the year: unaccountability. Powerful men survived when the evidence said they should not. Wrongdoing was documented and then, somehow, absorbed, digested, forgotten. Failure was rewarded with continuity. No senior head rolled that did not want to roll. The great machinery of consequence, which we are assured grinds slowly but exceedingly fine, turned out to be jammed.

Which leaves me with the two resolutions this chastening year has pressed upon me. First: do not expect the mechanisms of accountability to work. They are decorative more often than they are functional. Second, and more prudently: do not make predictions at all.

And yet the temptation is irresistible, so let me close with a reminder of just how ungovernable the world remains, how gloriously immune to the forecaster's craft. For who among us, twelve months ago, sharpening our pencils and consulting our crystal balls, could possibly have predicted that a cabinet minister - a serious man, a solemn man, a man photographed frequently beside flags - would end his year embroiled in a personal scandal so improbable, so operatic, so wholly beyond the reach of reasonable anticipation, that even the tabloids seemed briefly lost for words? Nobody saw it coming. Which is rather the point.

Happy New Year.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/train/3/train_31
body:
The guardians of our civic virtue have spoken, and the news, apparently, is good. First came the report into cash-for-passports, that grubby little affair in which a billionaire donor to the Millennium Dome suddenly found himself the proud holder of a British passport. The inquiry pored over the evidence, weighed the propriety of it all, and concluded that nothing "improper" had taken place. Nothing to see here. Then the parliamentary sleaze committee turned its attention to a certain cabinet minister, sifted through the charges laid against him, cleared him of very nearly all of them, and — when it found one minor violation it could not entirely wave away — declined to impose any punishment worth mentioning. And then there was Downing Street's little intervention on behalf of a billionaire arms broker, whose planning application seemed to enjoy a most attentive hearing from the very top of government. That, too, was probed, and found to be no more than an innocent concern about "timing".

So there we are. Three separate examinations by three sets of solemn, high-minded custodians of the public interest, and all three have delivered the same verdict: relax, everything is above board, the system worked exactly as it should. The rich did not get special treatment. The rules were followed to the letter.

And yet. If nothing wrong happened, why doesn't it feel right? Why does the whole business leave a taste in the mouth that no official exoneration can quite wash away? That is the question worth answering — and it is the question the inquiries themselves seem incapable of asking.

Let me offer the common-sense diagnosis, the one you will hear in any pub or bus queue in the land. Forget the reports, forget the careful language of "propriety". These are all versions of the same story: someone with a great deal of money got treatment that you or I could only dream of. Consider the passport case again — not the tabloid version, but the account contained within the very report that cleared everyone involved. It records that the billionaire's application was personally championed by the head of the immigration directorate. It records that the ordinary residency rules were bent so that his case could be processed at speed. It records that a roster of former prime ministers and senior ministers weighed in to lobby on the applicants' behalf. The report reads all this and calls it "proper". The public reads it and knows exactly what it is.

The pattern repeats itself, again and again, until it stops looking like coincidence and starts looking like a system. A Formula One boss makes a large donation to the governing party, and shortly afterwards that party's government grants motor racing a handy exemption from a ban on tobacco advertising. A party treasurer showers the same party with donations, and is subsequently elevated to the House of Lords. In each case, someone will produce a report, or a form of words, insisting that the two events were entirely unconnected, that all was "proper". The trouble is not that these people are lying, exactly. The trouble is that their definition of "proper" has become so elastic, so accommodating to the interests of the wealthy, that it no longer describes anything the rest of us would recognise as fairness. The definition is itself the problem. We need a new one.

So what is to be done? Consider, first, the fashionable remedy of state funding for political parties. There is much to be said for it. It would liberate our politicians from the indignity of grovelling before millionaires, tin cup in hand, and it would sever the queasy link between a large cheque and a peerage or a policy favour. But it would not have prevented the passport affair, because the billionaires in that case were not writing cheques to a political party at all — they were donating to a cultural project, the Millennium Dome. State funding cannot police that. And it carries its own hazards, chief among them the risk of starving new and insurgent political movements of the oxygen they need to grow, entrenching the established parties in perpetuity.

Consider, then, the alternative: better regulation, tougher parliamentary committees, sharper watchdogs. Here the problem is structural and almost comically plain. The very committee that has just investigated the cabinet minister was forced to abandon most of its charges for a simple reason: he refused to cooperate with it. They had no power to compel him, no teeth with which to bite. A watchdog that cannot summon evidence is no watchdog at all. The regulatory route, in other words, is not merely inadequate; it is designed to be inadequate, policed by the very class it is meant to police.

If politics cannot fix this, perhaps the law can. Strip away the specifics of passports and planning permissions and party donations, and you are left with a single, ancient offence: the violation of the principle that everyone should be equal under the law. There is, quite plainly, one rule for the rich and another for the rest of us. And here it is worth glancing across the Atlantic. The American constitution's 14th Amendment guarantees every citizen the "equal protection of the laws" — six words that have done extraordinary work. From Brown v Board of Education in 1954 onwards, they have allowed ordinary Americans to march into court and demand that the state stop treating one group more favourably than another. The judges, not the politicians, became the engine of change.

Britain now has its own version of that clause, and most people have no idea. Article 14 of the European Convention on Human Rights, absorbed into our own law through the Human Rights Act, prohibits discrimination on grounds of sex, race, colour — and, crucially, "any other status". And what is wealth if not a status? Imagine, then, the possibilities. Two ordinary citizens, their passport applications languishing in the usual queue, going to court to ask why a billionaire's was fast-tracked and theirs was not. A small developer complaining that his planning decision was left to gather dust while a billionaire's got the personal attention of Downing Street. A modest business owner asking why he was refused the very exemption granted to a wealthy donor.

Would such cases succeed? Keir Starmer, one of the country's leading human rights lawyers, confirms that discrimination against those with less money could indeed fall within the prohibitions of Article 14 — provided the claimant could assemble the evidence and prove the cases were genuinely comparable in every respect but wealth. That is a high bar, admittedly. But even where it could not be cleared entirely, the mere bringing of such a challenge would force the authority into the dock, compelling it to explain, in public and under scrutiny, precisely why it treated the rich man so much better than the poor one.

The experts always told us the Human Rights Act would change things, and here is how. In the United States, the Bill of Rights has long been the instrument by which citizens advanced the causes their elected representatives found too awkward, too costly, too politically dangerous to touch. Now it is Britain's turn. For all those sick to the back teeth of the fast track and the quiet favour, the discreet word in the right ear, the lesson is clear enough. The politicians are too compromised to save us; they are the problem, not the cure. It is time to go to court.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/test/4/test_132
body:
When I arrived in Washington to take up my duties as a correspondent, a good many years ago now, I was fortunate enough to receive some advice from the late Alistair Cooke. The hardest single thing for a British person to grasp about the American political system, Cooke told me, is how important judges and the courts are within it. We British, he went on, are perpetually deceived by the shared language and the shared assumptions into believing that Britain and America see the world through the same eyes. In fact we do not, and nowhere is the gulf wider than over the law.

I recalled that conversation this week, and not only because John McCain was in London to deliver the first Alistair Cooke memorial lecture, nor even because George Bush was crossing the Atlantic for the G8 summit. I recalled it because, at the very moment when the world's attention was supposedly focused on global poverty, on climate change and on Iraq, the subject that in fact dominated Washington had nothing whatever to do with any of these things.

The reason was the resignation, over the Fourth of July weekend, of Justice Sandra Day O'Connor from the United States Supreme Court. To a British eye, this may seem an arcane matter, of interest chiefly to constitutional lawyers. It is nothing of the kind. It is a potentially seismic national political event, at least as important in its own context as the election of a president or of a new Congress. The nine-justice Supreme Court sits at the very centre of the American system of government. There has been no vacancy on it for 11 years. And O'Connor was no ordinary justice. For a decade and a half she was the swing voter on a whole series of finely balanced decisions, shaping the lives of millions of Americans on questions such as abortion, race and religion. Her departure, in other words, does not merely fill a chair. It may tilt the whole balance of American public life.

That is why the American right sees this as an immense opportunity, and the American left as a moment of the direst threat. It is an open secret that conservative lobbies have for years made the reshaping of the court, in the aggressively conservative mould of Antonin Scalia, their overriding priority, with a view to dismantling the expanded role of the federal government that has existed since the New Deal. Bush, who has no further election to face, may well share that ambition. Yet there are countervailing forces too. Most Americans are decent, pragmatic people, and they are frequently offended by the right's culture wars, as the opinion polls show and as the Terry Schiavo case so recently and vividly reminded them. Republican candidates facing next year's mid-term elections may be distinctly uneasy about a president who nominates a culture-warrior to the bench. And Bush will come under pressure to nominate another woman, not least because he is a politician who, for all his conservatism, has rarely ignored the political middle ground on which O'Connor's cautious pragmatism played so well.

Now turn to Britain, and try a small general-knowledge question. Who is the newly appointed lord chief justice of England and Wales, taking over from Lord Woolf on October 1? I suspect that most readers will not know. The answer is Nicholas Phillips, Lord Phillips of Worth Matravers, who has presided over the court of appeal for the past five years. There were two further appointments announced at the same time: Sir Anthony Clarke as master of the rolls, and Sir Igor Judge — surely the finest name on the English bench — as head of the Queen's Bench Division. These are three of the most senior judicial appointments in the land. Yet they were announced quietly, in a press release which offered almost no information about the men themselves. They received the most cursory media attention. They appeared on no front pages. They prompted no follow-up coverage, and generated no public debate whatever about whether these were the right people, about where they stood on the great legal questions of the day, or about where their appointment might take the English courts.

This silence is striking, and it is eloquent. It is the more so because these were the first appointments made under the major changes introduced by the Constitutional Reform Act 2005. One might have expected that appointments of this kind, in a supposedly reformed system, would be marked in some way that signalled to the public that here were key figures in a new constitutional era. In the event, nothing of the sort occurred. The most senior appointments in the newly reformed judicial system were made exactly as they have always been made — unheralded, and with the minimum of openness.

Return, finally, to America. It is reported that some fifty million dollars will be spent in the battle to fill O'Connor's seat. Two other justices are in their 80s, and a third is a cancer sufferer, which means that this process may soon be repeated, with even greater intensity, even greater expense and even more epochal consequences. From a distance, all this can seem a travesty, even a corruption, of the proper business of selecting the senior members of an independent, merit-based judiciary. I understand that reaction, and I partly share it. And yet the American process at least recognises, explicitly and implicitly, the centrality of judges and of the law to the constitutional processes of modern democratic government. Britain, with rare and distinguished exceptions — Lord Woolf being one of them — remains overwhelmingly in denial about these connections. If we meekly concede that we have no right to know about the highest judges in the land, or to call their thinking to public account, then we vacate an important part of the public arena. And the chain of reasoning matters. Unless we can respect our judges, it is harder to respect the law; and unless we respect the law, it is harder to respect the whole system of government of which both the law and the judges are integral parts.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/test/4/test_138
body:
It has become the customary consolation of European liberals, faced with the modern American Republican party, to reach for the reassuring memory of what the Grand Old Party once was. And there is comfort in the recollection. For much of the 20th century the Republican party was a broad and, in its way, honourable coalition, split between two large and competing instincts. On the one hand stood the grand tradition of activist, compassionate conservatism – the tradition of Theodore Roosevelt, the trust-buster and conservationist, and later of Nelson Rockefeller, patrician, expansive, socially engaged. On the other stood the more modest, cautious tradition – of Dwight Eisenhower, who governed with restraint and mistrusted overreach, and of Richard Nixon, whose domestic instincts, for all his other faults, were often more moderate than his rhetoric.

That party is dead. Whatever it is that gathered in Madison Square Garden this week to renominate George Bush, it is not that party. Today's Republican party is a different kind of coalition altogether – an alliance, uneasy and largely unexamined, between a dominant and aggressive religious conservative activist wing that knows exactly what it wants, and a much larger moderate electorate that continues, against a good deal of evidence, to believe that the party remains essentially benign. The activists supply the energy and the agenda. The moderates supply the votes. The activists know this. The moderates, one suspects, do not.

Anyone who watched the convention this week can have been in little doubt about which wing was in charge. The orchestrated hostility towards John Kerry was not incidental to the proceedings; it was the point of them. Delegates walked the floor wearing adhesive bandages decorated with little purple hearts, a calculated mockery of the wounds Kerry received in Vietnam. From the platform, the tone was set not by the moderate faces paraded for the cameras but by the brutal set-pieces. Zell Miller, the Georgia senator and nominal Democrat, denounced Kerry as "wrong, weak and wobbly" in a speech whose venom would have embarrassed a more self-confident party.

The hypocrisy of all this is almost too obvious to require statement, but it must be stated nonetheless. Kerry, whatever else one thinks of him, went to Vietnam and was wounded there. Bush spent the war in the Texas Air National Guard, his attendance record a matter of some obscurity. Dick Cheney, asked years later why he had not served, replied with characteristic iciness that he had had "other priorities" in the 1960s. Yet it is these men, and their partisans, who now presume to mock the military record of an opponent who actually did the fighting.

The lesson to draw from this is not simply that the modern Republican party is hypocritical, though it is. The deeper lesson is about what the hypocrisy conceals. The reverence on display this week was never really reverence for military service as such – if it had been, Kerry's Purple Hearts would have commanded respect rather than ridicule. What the party wants is not to honour the soldier but to own the politics of defence and security outright, to hold them as an exclusive Republican possession from which Democrats are to be permanently barred. Service is honoured when it serves the party and derided when it does not.

Notable too was what did not happen at the convention. Not once, across four days of speeches, was there a word of criticism of the military itself – not even, remarkably, on the subject of Abu Ghraib, whose photographs had circled the world only months before. The armed forces, in the convention's telling, are beyond reproach, an object of unqualified veneration. One is tempted to conduct a thought experiment. Suppose Al Gore had been president on 11 September 2001. Would the Republicans have rallied loyally around him in the national emergency, as the Democrats broadly did around Bush? The suggestion is laughable. They would have blamed him, and behind him they would have blamed Bill Clinton. Indeed they blame Clinton still. Wander into a Barnes and Noble in Manhattan and you will find whole shelves given over to the proposition – Losing Bin Laden, Dereliction of Duty, Intelligence Failure, an entire literary genre devoted to the thesis that the calamity was really the fault of the previous Democratic administration.

This matters because it tells us what a President Kerry could expect. He would face not a loyal opposition but a vindictive one, an opposition whose reflex, at the first stumble, would be towards impeachment. The Republican party has learned, in the Clinton years, that this is a weapon it is prepared to use, and it has shown no sign of unlearning the lesson.

Half a century ago, in his farewell address, Eisenhower warned his countrymen against the "misplaced power" of what he called the military-industrial complex. It was a warning delivered by a Republican, and it was aimed, though he did not say so, at forces within his own party as much as anywhere. The modern Republican party is, more than anything, the political expression of precisely the phenomenon Eisenhower feared. It is a militarist party, and it wears its militarism openly, in the testosterone-fuelled chants of "U-S-A, U-S-A" that punctuated every rally, in the relish for confrontation, in the identification of national strength with the readiness to use force.

But the military-industrial power is only half the story. The other half is the religious imperative with which it has fused. Here Bush himself is the master practitioner. His technique is one of double-coding – of using language that sounds emollient and moderate to the general audience while signalling something quite specific and much harder to the religious right that hears its own vocabulary within his. To the uninitiated it is compassion; to the initiated it is a covenant. The moderate electorate hears the reassuring surface; the activist base hears the pledge beneath it.

Occasionally the mask slips, and the pledge is spoken plainly. At a private rally away from the main cameras, Senator Sam Brownback of Kansas declared, without euphemism, that America was engaged in a "culture war". The convention's closing prayer asked for divine guidance in the struggle against the "forces of evil". These were not coded messages. They meant exactly what they said.

And this is finally the most disquieting thing about the week. So much of the convention was Orwellian in the strict sense – double-speak, in which words were made to mean their opposites, in which a party of the wealthy posed as the friend of the ordinary voter and a party of confrontation posed as the party of unity. But not all of it. Some of it was perfectly candid. When they spoke of the culture war, when they invoked the forces of evil, when they mocked an opponent's wounds, they were telling the plain truth about themselves. Those were the moments that ought to alarm us most – not the lies, but the statements that meant precisely what they said.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_34
body:
Of the great electoral landslides that have punctuated British politics since the Victorian age, there have really only been three that could be called triumphs of the left. New Labour swept in on the tide of 1997, and repeated the feat, if with a slightly diminished ecstasy, in 2001. Labour, more famously and more consequentially, won its majority in 1945. And then, before either of these, there was the Liberal landslide of 1906. The first was won by Tony Blair, whose face still adorns a thousand seminar rooms. The second was won by Clement Attlee, now the object of an almost sacramental veneration. The third was won by a man of whom, I would wager, most educated people in this country could tell you almost nothing at all.

His name was Sir Henry Campbell-Bannerman - "CB" to those who knew him, and to the few who now remember him. It is roughly a century since he took office and inaugurated what remains, by some distance, the longest continuous stretch of left-of-centre government in British history: nearly 17 unbroken years, from the Liberal victory of 1906 to the collapse of the Lloyd George coalition in 1922. No other government of the British left has matched it. And though that era is habitually associated with Asquith and with Lloyd George - the two men who succeeded and eclipsed him - the pivotal victory that made it all possible belonged to Campbell-Bannerman and to no one else.

Consider the man first, because he is worth considering. Margot Asquith, no easy judge of anybody, called him "essentially a bon vivant, a boulevardier and a humorist" - qualities that have been notably scarce among British prime ministers since Disraeli, and are scarcer still today. His biographer John Wilson described a man who was easy-going, largely unburdened by ambition, and disposed by temperament to let well alone. He kept politics in proportion. He was no workaholic. He bore no grudges. In an age that mistakes exhaustion for seriousness, all of this can too easily be read as indolence.

That would be the wrong lesson entirely. CB was not an intellectual, but he held his Liberal convictions with the same quiet constancy he brought to his lifelong love of Balzac and Flaubert. In 1898 he defined Liberalism as well as anyone ever has: as self-government, as trust in the general good sense of mankind, as opposition to artificial privilege, and as the making of laws that avoid aggravating inequality and secure to every person a chance at a good and useful life. Read it again. It still reads well, more than a century on.

Three things, above all, make him admirable.

The first is the Boer War. The Liberal party very nearly tore itself apart over it, with much of the hierarchy under Lord Rosebery cheering the war on while the radicals denounced it. As new leader, CB at first sought the middle path, as leaders of divided parties must. But in 1901 he delivered the single most important anti-war speech of the era, condemning the Salisbury government's "methods of barbarism" in South Africa. It was a phrase of great moral force, and it cost him dear at the time. It also happened to be right.

The second is his attitude to the emerging Labour party. CB liked the Labour men, sympathised with their grievances, and did not regard them as an enemy to be crushed. Within months of taking office he delivered the Trades Disputes Act of 1906 - and, crucially, fought off Asquith's efforts to water it down, pushing a stronger version through cabinet. It became the cornerstone of 20th-century labour law. That is not the record of an idler.

The third is his political skill, which his colleagues consistently underrated. He led from the centre. He rebuilt a party shattered by defeat in 1900 into the winner of one of the largest landslides in modern history, converting a Tory majority of 134 into a Liberal majority of 130 - a swing rarely equalled before or since. He did it by exploiting his opponents' weaknesses, above all their disastrous flirtation with protectionist tariffs, and he did it without overpromising. Most of his colleagues thought his strategy wrong. The result vindicated him utterly.

In office, his government outperformed every expectation held of it. Had the Tory-dominated House of Lords not blocked so much of its legislation, CB would have secured education reform, the abolition of the multiple franchise, and trade union reform within his first year alone. Even hobbled as he was, ministers like Haldane, with his army reforms, and Lloyd George, with merchant shipping, proved themselves genuine reformers.

Enoch Powell once wrote that all political careers, unless cut off in midstream at a happy juncture, end in failure. He wrote it, as it happens, about Joseph Chamberlain. The axiom does not apply to Campbell-Bannerman except in the most literal sense: he died in Downing Street in early 1908, having resigned the premiership on his deathbed in favour of Asquith. He went out, in other words, at the very top. There are worse ways to be spared the general rule.

Which brings us, finally, to oblivion. CB has a rival for the title of Britain's forgotten prime minister in Andrew Bonar Law, who was rescued from obscurity chiefly by Robert Blake's fine biography, The Unknown Prime Minister. One suspects, too, that Alec Douglas-Home may in time recede still further into the shadows than he has already. But for now, and unjustly, the title of Britain's unknown prime minister belongs to the admirable and neglected Henry Campbell-Bannerman - the man who won the third great landslide of the left, and whom almost no one remembers.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_37
body:
On the morning of the 1983 general election, I fell into conversation with a Labour ward secretary in Hackney. A decent, dogged man, he had been out knocking on doors since dawn and, encouraged by what he had heard on the doorstep, he told me that Labour would win by 20 seats. I told him, as gently as I could, that the Conservatives would win by more than 100. He looked at me as though I had spat on Keir Hardie's grave. Later that night, of course, Margaret Thatcher was returned to Downing Street with a majority of 144. My secret was not clairvoyance, still less a superior grasp of the mood on the streets of Hackney. My secret was simply that I believed what the opinion polls were telling me.

I had the same experience, in almost mirror image, on election day in 1997. That afternoon I asked the Labour leader himself what he thought the result would be. He mused, cautiously, that perhaps Labour might scrape home by 50. More like 150, I told him. He gave me a wary smile, as though I were tempting fate. The result, we all know, was a landslide of 179. Once again my presumption rested on nothing more mysterious than a willingness to take the polls at their word. It is a habit that has served me better than instinct, better than doorstep anecdote, and infinitely better than the wishful thinking to which even the shrewdest politicians are prone.

Which brings me to the present. When people ask me, as they increasingly do, who is going to win the American presidential election on November 2, I tell them that John Kerry is winning. I say this not because I want Kerry to win, though I do, but because that is what the polls currently show, particularly in the battleground states where the contest will actually be decided. This is not a popular thing to say to certain American correspondents, whose emails arrive in my inbox with monotonous regularity to inform me that I am a fool, a coward, a communist and a good deal else that is unprintable in a family newspaper. I bear it stoically.

But here I must enter an important caveat, because it is one that the poll-haters and the poll-worshippers alike consistently ignore. To say that Kerry is ahead in the polls is not the same as predicting that Kerry will win. Polls are snapshots, not prophecies. They record where opinion stands at a given moment, not where it will stand on polling day. They carry margins of error. The election is still months away, and much may change. All that I can responsibly say is that the race is close and that Kerry is at present narrowly ahead. Anyone who tells you more than that is selling something.

Now, having sung the praises of the pollster's art, honesty compels me to admit that we poll-believers have some explaining to do. Consider two recent findings. A Guardian-ICM poll placed Iraq at the very bottom of a list of issues likely to influence how people cast their votes at the next election. Yet an FT-Mori poll, conducted at much the same time, found that defence and foreign affairs were the single most important issues facing the country. Respected observers, Peter Preston among them, have confessed themselves baffled by this apparent contradiction. How, they ask, can Iraq be simultaneously the most pressing concern of the nation and an irrelevance at the ballot box?

The confusion, I want to argue, is more apparent than real. And it matters, for the good health of our civic conversation, that we stamp out the two lazy conceits that hover around such moments: the conceit that polls cannot be trusted, and the conceit, equally corrosive, that polls can be made to say anything you like. Neither is true, and the belief that they are true impoverishes our understanding of ourselves.

The resolution lies, as it so often does, in the questions themselves. The two polls were not measuring the same thing.
