ICM presented respondents with a prompted list of issues and asked which would matter most in deciding how they would vote. Just 12% picked Iraq. The National Health Service topped that list, at 59%. Mori, by contrast, asked an unprompted question: what is the most important issue facing Britain today? To that question, "defence, foreign affairs and international terrorism" came top, at 24%, rising to 38% once other related concerns were folded in. Put like that, the mystery dissolves. The polls are not contradictory. They are entirely compatible. It is perfectly possible, and indeed perfectly sensible, for a person to regard a subject as the gravest issue confronting the nation while giving it very little weight when deciding how to mark their ballot paper.

We have been here before, and it is instructive to remember it. Throughout the 1980s, the polls repeatedly showed that unemployment was regarded as the most important issue facing the country, and that Labour was thought to have the best policies for tackling it. And yet, election after election, people voted Conservative. Why? Because when it came to the questions that actually determined their vote, questions about taxation, about economic competence, about who could be trusted to run the country, the Tories held the advantage. There was no contradiction in this, only the ordinary complexity of the human mind, which is quite capable of holding two thoughts at once.

Something very similar is at work today. Foreign affairs and terrorism may well be, in the abstract, the most important issues facing Britain. Iraq casts a long and troubling shadow. But when the moment comes to vote, most people vote on the things that touch their own lives most directly: health, education, their children's schools, the wages in their pockets, the security of their old age. That is not shallowness. It is how democratic peoples have always behaved. The polls capture both truths, the grand national anxiety and the intimate personal calculation, and they capture them accurately, provided we take the trouble to read them properly.

Which is precisely why, if you press me, I will tell you that Labour will win the next election. Not because I wish it, though I do not disguise that I do, but because that is what the polls, read carefully and honestly, are telling us. Just as they told me about Hackney in 1983, and about that wary Labour leader in 1997.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_39
body:
It is one of the more uncomfortable truths about political conviction that the harder you believe in something, the harder it becomes to say anything critical about it in public. True believers are, almost by definition, poor critics of the causes they hold dear. The believer knows the arguments of the sceptics only too well, has usually rehearsed them privately, and has decided, after due wrestling, to keep faith. To voice the doubts out loud, in the open, before the enemies of the cause, feels not merely disloyal but treacherous. And so conscience and loyalty are locked, as they always have been, in a war that never produces an easy or a lasting winner. Neither combatant is dishonourable. That is precisely what makes the struggle so hard.

Tony Blair's belief in the paramount importance of the alliance with the United States is a classic case study in the perils of such undifferentiated loyalty. It is a belief that runs deep, deeper perhaps than any other in his political make-up, and it is a belief that has served him, and arguably the country, tolerably well over many years. Yet it is a belief that is now being tested by events, and the strain is beginning to show. Blair's current difficulties over Iraq are, at bottom, a collision between an enduring instinct and a threatening reality. The instinct tells him that America must always be embraced. The reality tells him that this particular America is not the one he thought he was embracing.

Begin with the domestic. Blair has long held, and has said as much to those who know him well, that British politics runs downstream from America. He believed, and believed for good reason, that Bill Clinton's victory in 1992 and his re-election in 1996 were necessary preconditions of New Labour's triumph in 1997. With a Democrat in the White House, Labour could present itself as moving with the grain of history rather than against it. Progressive politics acquired a transatlantic respectability that it had conspicuously lacked in the Thatcher and Reagan years. To be modern, centrist and electorally serious was suddenly to be in tune with the most powerful nation on earth.

Which is exactly why the election of George W Bush in 2000 posed Blair such a problem. Overnight the current appeared to reverse. There was a real danger that the Conservatives — first under William Hague, later under Iain Duncan Smith — might claim to be the British party in tune with the new America, the natural cousins of the incoming Republicans. This is a large part of why Blair rushed, with almost unseemly haste, to be the first foreign leader received at Camp David in the early weeks of 2001. He was not merely paying court to a new president. He was staking a claim, planting a flag, denying his domestic opponents the ground on which they might otherwise have stood.

Over time, though, Blair's belief widened from the domestic to the international, and here it acquired the character of something like a doctrine. The United States, in this view, is the indispensable nation, the only power capable of settling the world's great problems. And the Anglo-American relationship is the catalyst that keeps America engaged with the world rather than retreating into the isolationism that has always been one of its deepest temptations. How much of the mystique of the "special relationship" Blair truly swallows, and how much he simply uses, remains genuinely ambiguous; for a man who so evidently identifies himself as a European, his emotional attachment to Washington can seem oddly overwrought. Yet the attachment is real. Those who dined at the White House with Clinton in 1998 recall Blair quoting the words that Harry Hopkins carried to Churchill, the biblical words of Ruth: "Whither thou goest I will go... Thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God. Even to the end." It is hard to hear that and doubt where his heart lay.

The trouble is that hearts and administrations are different things. Even under Clinton, Blair sometimes struggled to secure the American engagement he prized — Kosovo was a hard sell, and he had to work at it. But Bush presents a difficulty of an altogether different order. As Frances Fitzgerald observed in the New York Review of Books, this administration has broken with the internationalist premises that every postwar administration bar Reagan's first term had broadly accepted. Bush has rarely troubled to define his foreign-policy goals in anything resembling concrete terms. He speaks vaguely of threats, and of security solutions defined by America alone. One recalls his own campaign formulation, offered without embarrassment: "We're not so sure who the they are, but we know they're there." It is not the language of the moral internationalist order that Blair so eloquently invokes.

Nor is this merely a matter of the president's temperament. His subordinates have set an agenda that repudiates collective action root and branch. Dick Cheney, Donald Rumsfeld and, most candidly of all, John Bolton, have made no secret of it. "There is no such thing as the UN," Bolton has said, and the international community, on this view, is nothing more than what the United States can persuade others to follow when it happens to suit American interests. The Pentagon, meanwhile, has quietly shifted from what it used to call a "threat-based strategy" to a "capabilities-based approach" — a formulation that allows it to request whatever military spending it likes without troubling to justify it against any identifiable enemy. Rumsfeld talks, revealingly, of defending "against the unknown." It is difficult to build a moral internationalism on so studied an evasion.

This is the reality that keeps undermining Blair. He wants — sincerely, and in his own speeches at Brighton and to the TUC he has said so — to portray the Bush administration as a willing partner in exactly the kind of international order he champions. And one should have sympathy for him. He believes in the right things. He is trying, in his lonely and often thankless way, to exert the influence that only Britain, of all America's allies, is positioned to exert. The difficulty is not his goals. The difficulty is the yawning disjunction between what he wishes to believe about the administration and what is demonstrably true of it. And so he earns grief on both sides of the Atlantic — grief at home from those who see him deceived, grief in Washington from those who find his internationalism quaint.

Which returns us to where we began. This administration has discarded the very rules by which Blair wants to play. That is the plain fact of the matter. And rather than confront the fact, Blair prefers to behave as though it did not exist. It is the classic error of the over-loyalist, who cannot bring himself to speak the criticism his conscience already knows to be true, and who thereby lets loyalty override clarity. The believer keeps the faith. But the faith, in this instance, is placed in something that no longer answers to it.
